Variables created inside a function (including parameters) are local to that function. They exist only while the function is executing and cannot be accessed from outside the function. This prevents name conflicts and makes functions independent.

# Easy question (3.5): What happens if you try to print x outside the function?
# Uncomment the last line and see what happens.

def demo():
    x = 10
    print("Inside demo:", x)

demo()
# print(x)  # Uncomment and see what happens

Hard question (3.5)
Explain why local variables help avoid name conflicts in large programs.
Hint: Each function gets its own “private” namespace. The same variable name can be used safely in different functions without interfering.


3.6 Stack diagrams
A stack diagram shows the sequence of function calls, with each function call represented as a frame containing its local variables. When a function calls another, a new frame is added to the stack. When a function returns, its frame is removed. This helps visualize program execution flow.

# Easy question (3.6): Trace the order of prints.
# Which function returns first: f or g?

def f():
    print("In f")
    g()

def g():
    print("In g")

print("Start")
f()
print("End")

Hard question (3.6)
Describe the stack of function calls when f() calls g(), and g() calls h() before anything returns.
Hint: The stack grows: first f frame, then g frame on top, then h frame on top of that. They return in reverse order (LIFO - Last In First Out).


3.7 Tracebacks
A traceback is the error message Python prints when an exception occurs. It shows the chain of function calls that led to the error, with the most recent call at the bottom. Reading tracebacks helps you locate where bugs occur in your code.

# Easy question (3.7): This code will raise an error. Run it and observe the traceback.
# What information does the traceback provide?

def bad():
    return 1 / 0  # division by zero

bad()

Hard question (3.7)
How can reading a traceback help you find which function has a bug?
Hint: The traceback shows the call chain. The last part tells you the exact line and function where the error occurred. Work backwards to see how execution reached that point.


3.8 Why functions?
Functions provide several benefits: they enable code reuse (write once, use many times), improve organization (group related code together), reduce duplication (avoid copying code), make programs easier to read and maintain, and allow testing of individual pieces. Functions are fundamental building blocks of programs.

# Easy question (3.8): Write a small function to avoid repeating code.
# Create a function that prints "Hello" followed by a name.

def say_hello(___):
    print("Hello", ___)

say_hello("Alice")
say_hello("Bob")

Hard question (3.8)
Explain how functions improve both readability and testability of code.
Hint: Named chunks of code with clear inputs/outputs are easier to understand. They can also be tested independently, making debugging easier.

title: Python Programming Complete Quiz
text:
5. Conditionals and Recursion

5.1 Integer division and modulus
Integer division (//) divides two numbers and returns the floor (whole number part) of the result. The modulus operator (%) returns the remainder after division. Together, they are useful for working with whole numbers and checking divisibility.

# Easy question (5.1): Fill in the blanks to compute integer division and modulus.

quotient = 7 ___ 3  # use //
remainder = 7 ___ 3  # use %

print("Quotient:", quotient)
print("Remainder:", remainder)

Hard question (5.1)
How could you use % to test if a number is even?
Hint: An even number has no remainder when divided by 2. Use n % 2 == 0.


5.2 Boolean expressions
A Boolean expression evaluates to either True or False. Common Boolean expressions use comparison operators: == (equal), != (not equal), < (less than), > (greater than), <= (less than or equal), >= (greater than or equal). Boolean expressions are used in conditionals and loops.

# Easy question (5.2): Complete the Boolean expression.

result = 5 ___ 3  # use >, <, ==, etc.
print(result)  # Should print True

Hard question (5.2)
Write a Boolean expression that is True if x is between 1 and 10 (inclusive).
Hint: Use and to combine two conditions: x >= 1 and x <= 10.


5.3 Logical operators
Logical operators combine or modify Boolean expressions: and (both must be True), or (at least one must be True), not (negates the value). They allow you to build complex conditions from simpler ones.

# Easy question (5.3): Complete the logical expression.

result = True ___ False  # use and, or, not
print(result)  # Should print False

Hard question (5.3)
Write a condition that is True if age is at least 18 and country is 'US'.
Hint: Use and to combine: age >= 18 and country == 'US'.


5.4 if statements
An if statement executes a block of code only if its condition is True. The syntax is if condition: followed by an indented block. This allows programs to make decisions and execute different code based on conditions.

# Easy question (5.4): Write a simple if that prints "Positive" if x > 0.

x = 5
if ___:
    print("Positive")

Hard question (5.4)
Combine an if with an else to print "Positive" or "Non-positive" depending on x.
Hint: Use if x > 0: for the positive case, and else: for the non-positive case.


5.5 The else clause
The else clause provides an alternative block of code that executes when the if condition is False. It must follow an if statement and handles the case when the condition is not met. Together, if and else allow two-way branching.

# Easy question (5.5): Complete the if/else to print "Even" or "Odd".

n = 4
if n % 2 == 0:
    print(___)
else:
    print(___)

Hard question (5.5)
Write an if/else that prints "Even" if n is even, otherwise "Odd".
Hint: Use n % 2 == 0 to check if even, then print accordingly in each branch.


5.6 Chained conditionals
Chained conditionals use if, elif (else-if), and else to handle multiple cases. elif allows you to check additional conditions after the initial if. Only one branch executes - the first condition that is True. This enables multi-way branching.

# Easy question (5.6): Complete the chained conditional.

x = 0
if x > 0:
    print("positive")
___ x < 0:  # use elif
    print("negative")
else:
    print(___)

Hard question (5.6)
Write a chained conditional that prints "negative", "zero", or "positive" depending on the value of x.
Hint: Use if x < 0:, elif x == 0:, else: (for positive).


5.7 Nested conditionals
A nested conditional has an if statement inside another if block. This allows you to check multiple conditions in sequence. The inner if only executes if the outer condition is True. Nested conditionals enable complex decision-making logic.

# Easy question (5.7): Complete the nested conditional.

age = 20
has_id = True

if age >= 18:
    if ___:
        print("Allowed")
    else:
        print("No ID")
else:
    print("Too young")

Hard question (5.7)
Write nested if statements that first check if age >= 18, and inside that check if has_id is True before printing "Allowed".
Hint: Outer if age >= 18:, inner if has_id:, then print "Allowed".


5.8 Recursion
Recursion is when a function calls itself to solve a smaller version of the same problem. A recursive function must have a base case (stopping condition) and a recursive case (calls itself with modified input). Recursion is powerful for problems that have a natural recursive structure.

# Easy question (5.8): What is the key requirement for a recursive function to stop?
# Answer in a comment.

# A recursive function must have a ___ case.

Hard question (5.8)
Describe a recursive definition of the factorial function n! (factorial of n).
Hint: Base case: factorial(0) = 1 or factorial(1) = 1. Recursive case: factorial(n) = n * factorial(n-1).


5.9 Stack diagrams for recursive functions
When a recursive function calls itself, Python creates a new frame on the call stack for each call. Each frame has its own local variables. As recursion unwinds (returns), frames are removed from the stack. Stack diagrams help visualize how recursion builds up and unwinds.

# Easy question (5.9): When a recursive function calls itself, 
# does Python create a new frame on the stack? (Answer: Yes/No)

# Answer: ___

Hard question (5.9)
Explain what happens on the stack when fact(3) calls fact(2), which calls fact(1).
Hint: Stack grows: fact(3) frame → fact(2) frame → fact(1) frame. Then unwinds in reverse order as each returns.


5.10 Infinite recursion
Infinite recursion occurs when a recursive function never reaches a base case, causing it to call itself forever. This eventually causes a RecursionError when Python’s call stack limit is exceeded. To avoid infinite recursion, ensure every recursive path eventually reaches a base case.

# Easy question (5.10): What error do you typically see if recursion never stops?

# Answer: ___

Hard question (5.10)
Give an example (in words) of a recursive function definition that would cause infinite recursion.
Hint: A function that calls itself without modifying the input or checking a base case, like def bad(): return bad().

title: Python Programming Complete Quiz
text:
6. Return Values

6.1 Some functions have return values
Many functions use the return statement to send a value back to the caller. The returned value can be stored in a variable, used in expressions, or passed to other functions. Functions that return values are more useful than those that only perform side effects.

# Easy question (6.1): What keyword is used to send a value back from a function?

# Answer: ___

Hard question (6.1)
Write a function add(a, b) that returns the sum of a and b.
Hint: Use def add(a, b): followed by return a + b.


6.2 And some have None
If a function does not explicitly return a value (no return statement or just return without a value), it returns None by default. None is a special value representing “nothing”. This is important to remember when using function results in expressions.

# Easy question (6.2): What does print("hi") return?

result = print("hi")
print("Result:", result)  # What is result?

Hard question (6.2)
Explain why using the result of print() in an expression often causes bugs.
Hint: print() returns None, so operations like x = print("hi") + 5 will fail because you can’t add None to a number.


6.3 Return values and conditionals
Functions can have multiple return statements, each returning different values based on conditions. When a return executes, the function immediately exits. This allows functions to return different results depending on their input or internal state.

# Easy question (6.3): Can a function have more than one return statement?
# Answer: Yes/No

# Answer: ___

Hard question (6.3)
Write a function sign(x) that returns -1 for negative x, 0 for zero, and 1 for positive x.
Hint: Use if x < 0: return -1, elif x == 0: return 0, else: return 1.


6.4 Incremental development
Incremental development means building and testing your code in small steps. Start with a simple version, test it, then add features gradually. This approach helps catch bugs early and makes debugging easier. It’s better than writing everything at once and then trying to fix all problems.

# Easy question (6.4): Why is it helpful to test your code after each small change?
# Answer in a comment.

# Because ___

Hard question (6.4)
Describe how you would use incremental development to write and test a function that computes the average of a list of numbers.
Hint: Start by writing a function that sums the list, test it, then add division by length, test again, then handle edge cases like empty lists.


6.5 Boolean functions
A Boolean function returns True or False. These functions are useful for testing conditions and can be used directly in if statements. Boolean functions often have names starting with is_ or has_ to indicate they return a Boolean value.

# Easy question (6.5): What type of value does a Boolean function return?

# Answer: ___ or ___

Hard question (6.5)
Write a Boolean function is_even(n) that returns True if n is even and False otherwise.
Hint: Use return n % 2 == 0.


6.6 Recursion with return values
Recursive functions can return values that are combined as the recursion unwinds. The base case returns a value directly, and recursive cases combine the current value with the result of the recursive call. This allows recursive functions to compute and return results.

# Easy question (6.6): Does a recursive function always need a return statement?
# Answer: Yes/No

# Answer: ___

Hard question (6.6)
Explain how a recursive sum_list(lst) function could compute the sum of all elements in a list.
Hint: Base case: empty list returns 0. Recursive case: return lst[0] + sum_list(lst[1:]).


6.7 Leap of faith
The “leap of faith” is a mental approach to understanding recursion: assume the recursive call works correctly for smaller inputs, then reason about the current step. You don’t need to trace through every recursive call - trust that smaller cases work, then verify the current case combines them correctly.

# Easy question (6.7): Why is the "leap of faith" useful when understanding recursion?
# Answer in a comment.

# Because ___

Hard question (6.7)
Use the leap of faith to explain why a recursive factorial function correctly computes n!.
Hint: Assume factorial(n-1) correctly computes (n-1)!. Then factorial(n) = n * factorial(n-1) correctly computes n! because it multiplies n by the correct result for n-1.


6.8 Fibonacci
The Fibonacci sequence is defined by F(0) = 0, F(1) = 1, and F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2) for n > 1. Each number is the sum of the two preceding ones. Fibonacci numbers appear in nature and have many mathematical properties. They can be computed recursively or iteratively.

# Easy question (6.8): What are the first five Fibonacci numbers starting from F(0)?

# F(0) = ___, F(1) = ___, F(2) = ___, F(3) = ___, F(4) = ___

Hard question (6.8)
Describe a simple recursive definition of a function fib(n) that returns the nth Fibonacci number.
Hint: Base cases: fib(0) = 0, fib(1) = 1. Recursive case: fib(n) = fib(n-1) + fib(n-2).

title: Python Programming Complete Quiz
text:
7. Iteration and Search

7.1 Loops and strings
You can iterate over a string with a for loop, visiting each character in order. Strings are sequences, so you can loop through them character by character. This allows you to process, search, or modify strings programmatically.

# Easy question (7.1): What does this print?

for ch in "cat":
    print(ch)
# It prints: ___, ___, ___

Hard question (7.1)
Write a loop that counts how many times the letter 'a' appears in a string s.
Hint: Initialize a counter to 0, loop through s, and increment the counter when you find 'a'.


7.2 Reading the word list
You can read words from a file or list and process them one by one in a loop. Files can be opened and iterated over line by line. This allows you to process large amounts of text data efficiently.

# Easy question (7.2): What method do you call on a file object to get its lines one by one?
# Answer: ___

Hard question (7.2)
Sketch code that opens a file 'words.txt' and prints the first 10 words.
Hint: Use open('words.txt'), loop with enumerate or a counter, and break after 10 iterations.


7.3 Updating variables
In a loop, you often update variables, like increasing a counter: count = count + 1. The shorthand count += 1 does the same thing. Updating variables allows you to accumulate values, count occurrences, or track state as you iterate.

# Easy question (7.3): What is a common shorthand for x = x + 1?

x = 5
x ___ 1  # fill in the operator
print(x)  # Should print 6

Hard question (7.3)
Explain why count = count + 1 depends on the previous value of count.
Hint: The right side (count + 1) is evaluated first using the current value, then assigned back to count, updating it.


7.4 Looping and counting
Loops are often used to count items that meet a condition. You initialize a counter to 0, then increment it inside the loop when the condition is met. This pattern is very common for counting occurrences, filtering, or accumulating values.

# Easy question (7.4): What initial value do you usually give a counter variable?

count = ___  # fill in the initial value

Hard question (7.4)
Write a loop that counts how many numbers in a list nums are greater than 10.
Hint: Initialize count = 0, loop through nums, and increment count when num > 10.


7.5 The in operator
The in operator checks membership: for strings, it checks if a substring exists; for lists, it checks if an element exists; for dictionaries, it checks if a key exists. It returns True or False. The behavior differs slightly between types.

# Easy question (7.5): What does 'a' in 'cat' evaluate to?

result = 'a' ___ 'cat'  # use in
print(result)  # Should print True

Hard question (7.5)
Explain how in works differently for strings vs dictionaries.
Hint: For strings, in checks for substrings. For dictionaries, in checks for keys only, not values.


7.6 Search
A search scans through items to find one that matches a condition. You typically loop through the collection, check each item, and return or process the first match. You might use break to exit early when found, or return the index/position of the match.

# Easy question (7.6): What should a search loop do when it finds what it's looking for?
# Answer: ___ (use break, return, continue, etc.)

Hard question (7.6)
Describe how you would search a list for a value and return its index, or -1 if not found.
Hint: Loop with enumerate to get both index and value, return the index when found, return -1 after the loop if not found.

title: Python Programming Complete Quiz
text:
8. Strings and Regular Expressions

8.1 A string is a sequence
Strings behave like sequences: you can index them (s[0]), slice them (s[1:3]), get their length (len(s)), and loop over them. Each character has a position (index) starting from 0. This makes strings easy to manipulate programmatically.

# Easy question (8.1): What does "hello"[1] return?

result = "hello"[___]  # fill in the index
print(result)  # Should print 'e'

Hard question (8.1)
Write code that prints each character of a string s on a separate line.
Hint: Use a for loop: for ch in s: print(ch).


8.2 String slices
A slice s[start:end] returns a substring from index start up to but not including end. You can omit start (starts from beginning) or end (goes to end). Negative indices count from the end. Slicing creates a new string.

# Easy question (8.2): What does "python"[0:2] return?

result = "python"[___:___]
print(result)  # Should print 'py'

Hard question (8.2)
How can you use slicing to reverse a string s in one expression?
Hint: Use s[::-1] - the -1 step reverses the string.


8.3 Strings are immutable
Strings cannot be changed in place. You cannot assign to s[0] to change a character. Instead, you must create a new string using slicing, concatenation, or string methods. This immutability helps prevent bugs and makes strings safe to use as dictionary keys.

# Easy question (8.3): Can you assign to s[0] to change the first character?
# Try it and see what happens.

s = "hello"
# s[0] = 'H'  # Uncomment and see what error you get

Hard question (8.3)
How can you change the first character of a string s to 'X' using slicing and concatenation?
Hint: Use 'X' + s[1:] to create a new string with ‘X’ followed by the rest of s.


8.4 String comparison
Strings can be compared with <, >, ==, etc., using lexicographic (dictionary) order. Python compares strings character by character using Unicode values. Uppercase letters come before lowercase in ASCII/Unicode, which can affect comparisons.

# Easy question (8.4): Which is smaller: 'apple' or 'banana'?

result = 'apple' ___ 'banana'  # use < or >
print(result)  # Should print True

Hard question (8.4)
Explain how uppercase vs lowercase letters can affect string comparisons.
Hint: In ASCII/Unicode, uppercase letters have smaller values than lowercase, so 'A' < 'a' is True. Use .lower() or .upper() to normalize before comparing.


8.5 String methods
String methods like lower(), upper(), strip(), split(), join(), replace(), find(), etc., return new strings based on the original. Strings are immutable, so methods don’t modify the original - they create new strings. Methods are called with dot notation: s.method().

# Easy question (8.5): What does ' Hello '.strip() do?

s = ' Hello '
result = s.___()  # fill in the method name
print(result)  # Should print 'Hello'

Hard question (8.5)
How can you split a sentence into words, then join them back together with hyphens?
Hint: Use sentence.split() to split, then '-'.join(words) to join with hyphens.


8.6 Writing files
You can open a file in write mode ('w') and use write() or print() to save strings to disk. Opening in 'w' mode overwrites existing files. Use 'a' for append mode. Always close files or use with statements for automatic cleanup.

# Easy question (8.6): What mode string do you pass to open() to write to a file?

f = open('output.txt', ___)  # fill in the mode
f.write('Hello')
f.close()

Hard question (8.6)
Sketch code that opens 'output.txt' and writes the string 'Hello' followed by a newline.
Hint: Use open('output.txt', 'w'), then f.write('Hello\\n') or print('Hello', file=f).


8.7 Find and replace
You can find and replace substrings using the replace() method, which replaces all occurrences. For more control, you can use loops and string methods like find() or index(). The replace() method takes old and new strings and returns a new string.

# Easy question (8.7): What does 'banana'.replace('a', 'o') return?

result = 'banana'.replace(___, ___)
print(result)  # Should print 'bonono'

Hard question (8.7)
Explain how you might replace only the first occurrence of a substring in a string.
Hint: Use find() to locate the first occurrence, then use slicing to build a new string with the replacement.


8.8 Regular expressions (basics)
Regular expressions (regex) are patterns used to match strings. The re module provides regex functionality. Common patterns include . (any character), * (zero or more), + (one or more), \\d (digit), \\w (word character). Regex allows powerful string matching and searching.

# Easy question (8.8): Which module do you import to use regular expressions?

import ___  # fill in the module name

Hard question (8.8)
Give a simple regex that matches a string of exactly three digits.
Hint: Use \\d{3} or \\d\\d\\d to match exactly three digits.


8.9 String substitution
The re.sub() function can search and replace parts of strings using regex patterns. It takes a pattern, replacement string, and the string to search. This is more powerful than replace() because it uses patterns instead of literal strings.

# Easy question (8.9): What does re.sub do?
# Answer: It ___ strings using regex patterns.

Hard question (8.9)
Describe how you would use re.sub to replace all digits in a string with '*'.
Hint: Use re.sub('\\d', '*', s) where \\d matches any digit.

title: Python Programming Complete Quiz
text:
9. Lists

9.1 A list is a sequence
A list is an ordered, mutable collection of items written in square brackets, e.g. [1, 2, 3]. Lists can contain items of any type, including mixed types. Like strings, lists are sequences - you can index, slice, and loop over them. Lists are one of Python’s most versatile data structures.

# Easy question (9.1): What is the length of [1, 2, 3]?

my_list = [1, 2, 3]
length = ___(my_list)  # fill in the function
print(length)  # Should print 3

Hard question (9.1)
Write code that prints each element of a list items on a separate line.
Hint: Use a for loop: for item in items: print(item).


9.2 Lists are mutable
List elements can be changed in place. You can assign to nums[0] to change the first element. Lists can grow (with append(), extend()) or shrink (with remove(), pop()). This mutability makes lists powerful but requires care to avoid unintended side effects.

# Easy question (9.2): How do you change the first element of nums to 10?

nums = [1, 2, 3]
nums[___] = ___  # fill in the index and value
print(nums)  # Should print [10, 2, 3]

Hard question (9.2)
Explain the difference between modifying a list in place and creating a new list.
Hint: In-place modification changes the original list (e.g., nums[0] = 10). Creating a new list leaves the original unchanged (e.g., new_nums = nums + [4]).


9.3 List slices
List slices work like string slices, returning a new list. lst[1:3] returns elements from index 1 up to (but not including) 3. Slicing creates a shallow copy - a new list containing references to the same objects. You can use slices to extract portions of lists.

# Easy question (9.3): What does [1, 2, 3, 4][1:3] return?

result = [1, 2, 3, 4][___:___]
print(result)  # Should print [2, 3]

Hard question (9.3)
How can you make a shallow copy of an entire list using slicing?
Hint: Use lst[:] or lst[0:len(lst)] to create a copy of the entire list.


9.4 List operations
You can use + (concatenation), * (repetition), in (membership), and other operators with lists. [1, 2] + [3, 4] creates a new list [1, 2, 3, 4]. [0] * 5 creates [0, 0, 0, 0, 0]. Be careful with [[]] * 3 - it creates a list with three references to the same inner list!

# Easy question (9.4): What does [1, 2] + [3, 4] return?

result = [1, 2] ___ [3, 4]  # use +
print(result)  # Should print [1, 2, 3, 4]

Hard question (9.4)
Explain what [0] * 5 returns and why [[]] * 3 can be dangerous.
Hint: [0] * 5 creates [0, 0, 0, 0, 0] (five independent zeros). [[]] * 3 creates three references to the same list, so modifying one affects all.


9.5 List methods
Common list methods include append(item) (adds to end), extend(iterable) (adds all items), insert(index, item) (inserts at position), remove(item) (removes first occurrence), pop(index) (removes and returns item), sort() (sorts in place), reverse() (reverses in place). Methods modify the list in place.

# Easy question (9.5): What does nums.append(5) do to the list nums?

nums = [1, 2, 3]
nums.___(5)  # fill in the method name
print(nums)  # Should print [1, 2, 3, 5]

Hard question (9.5)
Describe the difference between append and extend.
Hint: append adds a single item (even if it’s a list, it adds the whole list as one element). extend adds all items from an iterable individually.


9.6 Lists and strings
You can convert between lists and strings. list('cat') creates ['c', 'a', 't']. ' '.join(['hello', 'world']) creates 'hello world'. 'a,b,c'.split(',') creates ['a', 'b', 'c']. These conversions are useful for text processing.

# Easy question (9.6): What does list('cat') return?

result = list(___)  # fill in the string
print(result)  # Should print ['c', 'a', 't']

Hard question (9.6)
How can you split a comma-separated string into a list of values?
Hint: Use s.split(',') to split on commas. You might also want to use strip() to remove whitespace from each element.


9.7 Looping through a list
You can iterate over lists with for item in items:. To get both index and value, use enumerate(items). Looping is fundamental for processing list elements, transforming data, filtering, or accumulating results.

# Easy question (9.7): Write a loop that prints all elements of nums.

nums = [1, 2, 3]
for ___ in ___:
    print(___)

Hard question (9.7)
How would you loop over both the index and the value of a list at the same time?
Hint: Use enumerate: for index, value in enumerate(lst): to get both index and value in each iteration.


9.8 Sorting lists
Lists can be sorted in place with sort() or you can use sorted() to create a new sorted list. sort() modifies the original list and returns None. sorted() returns a new list and leaves the original unchanged. Both accept a key parameter for custom sorting.

# Easy question (9.8): What does nums.sort() do?

nums = [3, 1, 2]
nums.___()  # fill in the method name
print(nums)  # Should print [1, 2, 3]

Hard question (9.8)
Explain the difference between sorted(nums) and nums.sort().
Hint: sorted(nums) returns a new sorted list, leaving nums unchanged. nums.sort() modifies nums in place and returns None.


9.9 Objects and values
Different variables can refer to the same object in memory. After a = [1, 2] and b = a, both a and b refer to the same list object. Changes through one variable affect the other. You can check identity with is or id().

# Easy question (9.9): After a = [1, 2] and b = a, do a and b refer to the same list object?
# Answer: Yes/No

a = [1, 2]
b = a
# Answer: ___

Hard question (9.9)
How can you check whether two variables refer to the same object in memory?
Hint: Use a is b (checks identity) or id(a) == id(b) (compares memory addresses).


9.10 Aliasing
Aliasing occurs when two names refer to the same object. If a and b alias the same list, changes made through a (like a.append(3)) are visible through b. This can cause unexpected bugs when passing lists to functions, since the function can modify the original list.

# Easy question (9.10): If a and b alias the same list, what happens to b when you append to a?

a = [1, 2]
b = a
a.append(3)
print(b)  # What does b contain? ___

Hard question (9.10)
Why can aliasing cause unexpected bugs when passing lists to functions?
Hint: Functions receive a reference to the list, not a copy. If the function modifies the list, the caller sees the changes, which may be unintended.


9.11 List arguments
Passing a list as a function argument passes a reference to the list object, not a copy. This means the function can modify the original list. If you want to protect the original, pass a copy (using slicing lst[:] or list(lst) or copy.copy(lst)).

# Easy question (9.11): If a function appends to a list parameter, does the caller see the change?
# Answer: Yes/No

def add_item(lst):
    lst.append(4)

my_list = [1, 2, 3]
add_item(my_list)
print(my_list)  # What does this print? ___

Hard question (9.11)
How can you protect a list from being modified by a function that you call?
Hint: Pass a copy: function(lst[:]) or function(list(lst)) or function(copy.copy(lst)).


9.12 Making a word list
You can build a list of words by reading from a file (splitting lines), splitting a string, or processing text data. Common patterns include reading a file line by line, splitting each line into words, and accumulating them into a list. This is useful for text processing and analysis.

# Easy question (9.12): What string method could you use to split a sentence into words?

sentence = "hello world"
words = sentence.___()  # fill in the method name
print(words)  # Should print ['hello', 'world']

Hard question (9.12)
Describe how to read all words from a file into a list, one word per element.
Hint: Open the file, loop through lines, split each line into words, and extend or append to a result list. Or use list comprehension: [word for line in f for word in line.split()].

title: Python Programming Complete Quiz
text:
10. Dictionaries

10.1 A dictionary is a mapping
A dictionary maps keys to values, written as {key: value, ...}. Dictionaries are unordered (in Python < 3.7) or insertion-ordered (Python 3.7+). Keys must be immutable (strings, numbers, tuples). Dictionaries provide fast lookup by key and are ideal for representing relationships between data.

# Easy question (10.1): What type of brackets are used to write a dictionary literal?

d = {___: ___, ___: ___}  # fill in brackets and example key-value pairs
print(type(d))

Hard question (10.1)
Give an example of a dictionary that maps student names to their grades.
Hint: {'Alice': 95, 'Bob': 87, 'Charlie': 92} or similar.


10.2 Creating dictionaries
You can create dictionaries with literals {}, the dict() constructor, dictionary comprehensions, or by starting empty and adding items. dict(key1=value1, key2=value2) is another way to create dictionaries. Empty dictionaries use {}.

# Easy question (10.2): How do you create an empty dictionary?

d = ___
print(d)

Hard question (10.2)
Show two different ways to create the same dictionary: one with {} syntax and one with dict(...).
Hint: {'a': 1, 'b': 2} and dict(a=1, b=2) create the same dictionary.


10.3 The in operator
For dictionaries, in checks whether a key is present, not a value. 'name' in person checks if 'name' is a key in person. To check for values, you need value in person.values(). This is different from lists where in checks for elements.

# Easy question (10.3): What does 'name' in person check, where person is a dictionary?

person = {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 30}
result = 'name' ___ person  # use in
print(result)  # Should print True

Hard question (10.3)
How can you safely get the value for a key that might not exist in the dictionary?
Hint: Use d.get(key, default) which returns the default if the key doesn’t exist, or check if key in d: first.


10.4 A collection of counters
Dictionaries are often used as counters, mapping items to how many times they appear. You initialize counters to 0 for new keys, then increment them. The pattern counts[item] = counts.get(item, 0) + 1 is common. This is useful for frequency counting, histograms, and tallying.

# Easy question (10.4): What default value do you typically use when starting a counter for a new key?

counts = {}
counts['a'] = counts.get('a', ___) + 1  # fill in the default
print(counts)

Hard question (10.4)
Describe how you would count the frequency of each character in a string using a dictionary.
Hint: Loop through the string, use counts[ch] = counts.get(ch, 0) + 1 for each character.


10.5 Looping and dictionaries
You can loop over dictionaries in several ways: for key in d: (keys), for value in d.values() (values), for key, value in d.items() (key-value pairs). The items() method is most common when you need both keys and values.

# Easy question (10.5): What does for key in d: iterate over by default?

d = {'a': 1, 'b': 2}
for key in d:
    print(key)  # It iterates over: ___

Hard question (10.5)
How can you loop over both keys and values at the same time?
Hint: Use for key, value in d.items(): to unpack both key and value in each iteration.


10.6 Lists and dictionaries
Dictionaries and lists can be combined in powerful ways. A dictionary can have lists as values (e.g., {'alice': [95, 87, 92]}). A list can contain dictionaries. You can nest these structures to represent complex data like student records, course enrollments, or hierarchical data.

# Easy question (10.6): What type is students['alice'] likely to be if it stores a list of her grades?

students = {'alice': [95, 87, 92]}
result = students['alice']
print(type(result))  # It is a ___

Hard question (10.6)
Describe a structure that maps each course name to a list of enrolled student names.
Hint: {'Math': ['Alice', 'Bob'], 'Science': ['Alice', 'Charlie']} - a dictionary where keys are courses and values are lists of student names.


10.7 Accumulating a list
Dictionaries can accumulate lists by appending to the list for each key. This pattern groups items by some key. For example, you might group words by their first letter: groups[word[0]].append(word). This requires initializing empty lists for new keys first.

# Easy question (10.7): What method adds an element to the end of a list stored in a dictionary value?

groups = {'a': []}
groups['a'].___(1)  # fill in the method name
print(groups)

Hard question (10.7)
Explain how to build a dictionary that groups words by their first letter.
Hint: Loop through words, use word[0] as the key, initialize empty list if key doesn’t exist, then append the word to that list.

title: Python Programming Complete Quiz
text:
11. Tuples

11.1 Tuples are like lists
A tuple is an ordered collection like a list but written with parentheses, e.g. (1, 2, 3). Tuples can contain items of any type. Like lists, tuples are sequences - you can index, slice, and loop over them. The main difference is that tuples are immutable (cannot be changed).

# Easy question (11.1): How do you write a tuple containing the numbers 1, 2, and 3?

t = (___, ___, ___)  # fill in the tuple
print(t)

Hard question (11.1)
Why might you choose a tuple instead of a list?
Hint: Tuples are immutable, so they can be used as dictionary keys, are safer for data that shouldn’t change, and can indicate that the data structure is fixed.


11.2 But tuples are immutable
Tuples cannot be changed after creation. You cannot assign to t[0] to change an element. You cannot add or remove elements. This immutability makes tuples hashable (can be dictionary keys), prevents accidental modification, and can improve performance in some cases.

# Easy question (11.2): Can you assign to t[0] if t is a tuple?
# Try it and see what happens.

t = (1, 2, 3)
# t[0] = 10  # Uncomment and see what error you get

Hard question (11.2)
Give an example where tuple immutability is an advantage.
Hint: Tuples can be dictionary keys (lists cannot), or used to represent fixed data like coordinates (x, y) that shouldn’t accidentally change.


11.3 Tuple assignment
You can unpack tuples on the left-hand side of an assignment: x, y = (1, 2). This assigns 1 to x and 2 to y. Tuple assignment is useful for swapping variables (a, b = b, a) or returning multiple values from functions. The number of variables must match the tuple length.

# Easy question (11.3): What does x, y = (1, 2) assign to x and y?

x, y = (___, ___)  # fill in the tuple
print("x =", x, "y =", y)

Hard question (11.3)
Show how to swap a and b using tuple assignment in one line.
Hint: Use a, b = b, a - Python evaluates the right side first, then assigns to the left.


11.4 Tuples as return values
Functions can return tuples to provide multiple values at once. The caller can unpack the tuple: x, y = function(). This is cleaner than returning a list when you know exactly how many values there are. Common examples include returning coordinates, pairs, or multiple computed values.

# Easy question (11.4): Can a function return both a quotient and remainder using a tuple?
# Answer: Yes/No

# Answer: ___

Hard question (11.4)
Write a function that takes two numbers and returns both their sum and product as a tuple.
Hint: def sum_and_product(a, b): return (a + b, a * b).


11.5 Argument packing
Using *args in a function definition, you can pack multiple positional arguments into a tuple. *args collects all extra positional arguments. This allows functions to accept variable numbers of arguments. Inside the function, args is a tuple containing all the arguments.

# Easy question (11.5): What does *args collect inside a function definition?

def func(*args):
    print(type(args))  # args is a ___

func(1, 2, 3)

Hard question (11.5)
Write a function that takes any number of numeric arguments and returns their sum using *args.
Hint: def sum_all(*args): return sum(args) or loop through args and accumulate.


11.6 Zip
The zip() function combines multiple sequences into tuples of corresponding elements. zip([1, 2], ['a', 'b']) returns an iterator yielding (1, 'a'), (2, 'b'). zip stops when the shortest sequence ends. It’s useful for iterating over multiple sequences simultaneously.

# Easy question (11.6): What does list(zip([1, 2], ['a', 'b'])) return?

result = list(zip([___, ___], [___, ___]))
print(result)  # Should print [(1, 'a'), (2, 'b')]

Hard question (11.6)
How could you use zip to iterate over two lists names and scores together?
Hint: for name, score in zip(names, scores): to get corresponding pairs.


11.7 Comparing and sorting
Tuples can be compared and sorted lexicographically (element by element). (1, 2) < (1, 3) is True because the first elements are equal and the second element of the first tuple is smaller. Tuples are often used as sort keys to sort by multiple criteria.

# Easy question (11.7): Which comes first: (1, 2) or (1, 3)?

result = (1, 2) ___ (1, 3)  # use < or >
print(result)  # Should print True

Hard question (11.7)
Explain how tuples can be used as sort keys when sorting a list of records.
Hint: Use sorted(records, key=lambda r: (r.field1, r.field2)) to sort by multiple fields - first by field1, then by field2.


11.8 Inverting a dictionary
To invert a dictionary, you create a new dictionary whose keys are the original values and whose values are the original keys. This only works if the original values are immutable (hashable). If multiple keys share the same value, you need to handle that (e.g., make values lists).

# Easy question (11.8): What does it mean to invert a dictionary?
# Answer: To create a new dictionary where ___ become keys and ___ become values.

Hard question (11.8)
Describe how you would handle the case where multiple keys share the same value when inverting.
Hint: Make the inverted dictionary values lists: {value: [key1, key2, ...]}. Loop through original dict, append keys to lists.

title: Python Programming Complete Quiz
text:
13. Files

13.1 Filenames and paths
A filename may include a path describing where it is in the filesystem. Paths can be absolute (from the root) or relative (from the current directory). On Windows, paths use backslashes \\; on Unix/Mac, they use forward slashes /. The os.path module helps work with paths cross-platform.

# Easy question (13.1): What character is commonly used to separate folders in a Windows path?
# Answer: ___

Hard question (13.1)
Explain the difference between an absolute and a relative path.
Hint: Absolute paths start from the root (/home/user/file.txt or C:\\Users\\file.txt). Relative paths are relative to the current directory (file.txt or ../file.txt).


13.2 f-strings
f-strings (formatted string literals) are string literals prefixed with f that allow expressions inside {}. f"Hello, {name}" evaluates name and inserts it. f-strings support formatting: f"{pi:.2f}" formats to 2 decimal places. They’re the preferred way to format strings in Python 3.6+.

# Easy question (13.2): What does name = 'Ada'; f"Hello, {name}" evaluate to?

name = 'Ada'
result = f"Hello, {___}"  # fill in the variable
print(result)  # Should print 'Hello, Ada'

Hard question (13.2)
Show how to format a number pi to 3 decimal places using an f-string.
Hint: Use f"{pi:.3f}" - the .3f means 3 decimal places for a float.


13.3 YAML
YAML (YAML Ain’t Markup Language) is a human-friendly data serialization format often used for configuration files. It’s more readable than JSON for complex nested structures. Python can read/write YAML using the pyyaml library. YAML supports lists, dictionaries, strings, numbers, and nested structures.

# Easy question (13.3): Is YAML more like a programming language or a data description format?
# Answer: ___

Hard question (13.3)
Name one advantage of YAML over plain text configuration files.
Hint: YAML supports structured data (lists, dictionaries), is human-readable, and can represent nested hierarchies clearly.


13.4 Shelve
The shelve module provides a persistent dictionary-like object. It stores Python objects in a file, allowing you to save and retrieve data between program runs. shelve uses a database file (like dbm) and can store any picklable Python object. It’s simpler than a full database but more powerful than plain files.

# Easy question (13.4): What type of standard library module is shelve (what is it used for)?
# Answer: It is used for ___

Hard question (13.4)
Explain how you might store and later retrieve a dictionary of user settings using shelve.
Hint: import shelve, db = shelve.open('settings'), db['theme'] = 'dark', db.close(). Later: db = shelve.open('settings'), theme = db['theme'].


13.5 Storing data structures
You can serialize (save) complex data structures to files using formats like JSON, YAML, or modules like pickle and shelve. JSON is text-based and language-agnostic. pickle is Python-specific and can store any Python object. Choose based on your needs: portability (JSON) vs flexibility (pickle).

# Easy question (13.5): Why might you want to store a list or dictionary in a file?
# Answer: To ___ data between program runs.

Hard question (13.5)
Describe one risk of using pickle for untrusted data.
Hint: pickle can execute arbitrary code when loading files, making it unsafe for data from untrusted sources. Use JSON or validate data instead.


13.6 Checking for equivalent files
You can compare files by size, content, or checksums (hashes like MD5, SHA256). Comparing only filenames is not enough - files with the same name can have different content. Checksums provide a reliable way to detect if files are identical. The hashlib module provides hash functions.

# Easy question (13.6): Besides file size, what is one thing you can compare to check if two files are equivalent?
# Answer: ___

Hard question (13.6)
Explain why comparing only filenames is not enough to determine if two files are equivalent.
Hint: Files can have the same name but different content, or different names but identical content. Content comparison (checksums) is needed.


13.7 Walking directories
os.walk() lets you traverse all files and folders under a directory recursively. It yields tuples of (dirpath, dirnames, filenames) for each directory. This is useful for finding files, processing directory trees, or building file listings. It’s more powerful than os.listdir() which only lists one level.

# Easy question (13.7): Which module provides walk for iterating over directories?

import ___  # fill in the module name

Hard question (13.7)
Describe how you would use os.walk to print all .txt files under a folder.
Hint: for root, dirs, files in os.walk('folder'): then check if file.endswith('.txt'): for each file.


13.8 json
The json module reads and writes data in JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) format. JSON maps closely to Python: objects become dictionaries, arrays become lists. json.dumps() converts Python to JSON string; json.loads() converts JSON string to Python. json.dump() and json.load() work with files. JSON is widely used for data exchange.

# Easy question (13.8): What function would you use to convert a Python dictionary to a JSON string?

import json
d = {'a': 1, 'b': 2}
result = json.___(d)  # fill in the function name
print(result)

Hard question (13.8)
Explain how JSON differs from Python syntax for dictionaries.
Hint: JSON uses double quotes for strings (Python allows single), JSON keys must be strings (Python allows any hashable type), JSON doesn’t support comments or trailing commas.

title: Python Programming Complete Quiz
text:
14. Classes and Functions

14.1 Programmer-defined types
You can define new types (classes) with class Name: to represent structured data. Classes allow you to bundle data (attributes) and behavior (methods) together. This is object-oriented programming. Classes help organize code and model real-world concepts.

# Easy question (14.1): What keyword starts a class definition?

class ___:  # fill in the keyword
    pass

Hard question (14.1)
Why might you define your own class instead of using only built-in types?
Hint: Classes let you bundle related data and behavior, create domain-specific abstractions, and organize code better than using only dictionaries and functions.


14.2 Attributes
Attributes are variables stored inside objects, accessed with dot notation like obj.name. You can assign attributes directly: obj.x = 10. Attributes store the state (data) of an object. Different instances of the same class have their own attribute values.

# Easy question (14.2): How do you access the attribute x of an object p?

p.x = 10
value = p.___  # fill in how to access x
print(value)

Hard question (14.2)
Explain the difference between an attribute and a local variable.
Hint: Attributes belong to an object and persist as long as the object exists. Local variables exist only within a function call and are destroyed when the function returns.


14.3 Objects as return values
Functions can create objects and return them. This allows functions to construct and return complex data structures. For example, a function might create a Point object with given coordinates and return it. This is common in object-oriented design.

# Easy question (14.3): Can a function return a newly created object?
# Answer: Yes/No

# Answer: ___

Hard question (14.3)
Describe how a function could return a new Point object with given coordinates.
Hint: def create_point(x, y): p = Point(); p.x = x; p.y = y; return p or use __init__ and return Point(x, y).


14.4 Objects are mutable
Objects of custom classes are usually mutable - their attributes can change over time. You can modify obj.x after creation. This mutability allows objects to represent state that changes. However, it requires care to avoid unintended side effects when objects are shared.

# Easy question (14.4): How do you change the x attribute of a Point object p to 10?

p.x = ___
print(p.x)

Hard question (14.4)
Why can mutability make reasoning about object state more complex?
Hint: Objects can change unexpectedly if shared between functions, making it harder to track state and debug. Immutable objects are easier to reason about.


14.5 Copying
Copying objects can be shallow (copy references to nested objects) or deep (recursively copy nested objects too). The copy module provides copy.copy() (shallow) and copy.deepcopy() (deep). Shallow copies share nested objects; deep copies create independent copies of everything.

# Easy question (14.5): What module provides copy and deepcopy functions?

import ___

Hard question (14.5)
Explain a situation where a shallow copy is not enough and you need a deep copy.
Hint: If an object contains nested mutable objects (like a list), shallow copy shares those nested objects. Changing them affects both copies. Deep copy creates independent copies.


14.6 Pure functions
A pure function depends only on its inputs and has no side effects (doesn’t modify global state or arguments). Given the same inputs, it always returns the same output. Pure functions are easier to test, reason about, and debug. They’re the foundation of functional programming.

# Easy question (14.6): Does a pure function modify any global state?
# Answer: Yes/No

# Answer: ___

Hard question (14.6)
Why are pure functions easier to test and reason about?
Hint: Pure functions have no hidden dependencies or side effects. You can test them in isolation, and the same inputs always produce the same outputs, making behavior predictable.


14.7 Prototype and patch
“Prototype and patch” is a development approach: start with a simple working version (prototype), then add features incrementally (patch). This is quick for getting something working but can lead to messy code. It’s often better to plan the design first, but prototyping helps explore ideas.

# Easy question (14.7): What does it mean to "prototype" a class?
# Answer: To create a ___ version first.

Hard question (14.7)
Why might patching classes at runtime make code harder to understand?
Hint: Runtime modifications can make it unclear what a class actually does, break assumptions, and make debugging harder since the class definition doesn’t match its actual behavior.

title: Python Programming Complete Quiz
text:
15. Classes and Methods

15.1 Defining methods
Methods are functions defined inside a class that operate on instances. They take self as the first parameter (conventionally named self), which refers to the instance. Methods are called with dot notation: obj.method(). They can access and modify the object’s attributes via self.

# Easy question (15.1): What is the conventional name of the first parameter of an instance method?

class Point:
    def move(self, dx, dy):  # The first parameter is ___
        self.x += dx
        self.y += dy

Hard question (15.1)
Write a simple Point class with a method move(dx, dy) that adds to x and y.
Hint: class Point: def __init__(self, x, y): self.x = x; self.y = y; def move(self, dx, dy): self.x += dx; self.y += dy.


15.2 Another method
You can define multiple methods in a class to provide different behaviors. Each method can access self to work with the object’s attributes. Methods can call other methods using self.method_name(). This allows you to build complex behaviors from simpler ones.

# Easy question (15.2): Can a class have more than one method?
# Answer: Yes/No

# Answer: ___

Hard question (15.2)
Explain how two methods of the same class might share or reuse code.
Hint: Methods can call other methods via self.method_name(), allowing code reuse. Common functionality can be extracted into helper methods.


15.3 Static methods
@staticmethod defines a method that does not receive self and behaves like a plain function inside the class namespace. Static methods are called on the class: ClassName.static_method() or on instances. They’re useful for utility functions related to the class but not needing instance data.

# Easy question (15.3): Do static methods receive self automatically?
# Answer: Yes/No

# Answer: ___

Hard question (15.3)
When would you choose a @staticmethod instead of a regular method?
Hint: When the function doesn’t need instance data (self) but is logically related to the class, like utility functions or alternative constructors.


15.4 Comparing Time objects
You can define comparison methods (__lt__, __eq__, __ne__, __gt__, __le__, __ge__) so objects like Time can be compared. __lt__ defines <, __eq__ defines ==. Implementing these allows sorting and using objects in sets/dictionaries.

# Easy question (15.4): What special method is used for "less than" comparisons?

class Time:
    def __lt__(self, other):  # This method is called ___
        return self.total_seconds() < other.total_seconds()

Hard question (15.4)
Explain how implementing __lt__ allows sorting a list of Time objects.
Hint: sorted() uses __lt__ to compare elements. Once __lt__ is defined, Python can sort the list automatically.


15.5 The str method
__str__ returns a human-readable string representation of an object. It’s called automatically by print() and str(). If not defined, Python uses a default representation. __str__ should return a string that clearly represents the object’s state.

# Easy question (15.5): When is __str__ called automatically?
# Answer: When you use ___ or ___

# Answer: When you use print() or str()

Hard question (15.5)
How would you implement __str__ for a Point(x, y) to return "(x, y)"?
Hint: def __str__(self): return f"({self.x}, {self.y})" or use string formatting.


15.6 The init method
__init__ is a special method called when you create an instance. It initializes the object’s attributes. p = Point(1, 2) calls Point.__init__(p, 1, 2). __init__ doesn’t return a value (it returns None). It’s the constructor that sets up new objects.

# Easy question (15.6): What method is called when you write p = Point(1, 2)?

class Point:
    def __init__(self, x, y):  # This method is called ___
        self.x = x
        self.y = y

Hard question (15.6)
Explain how you would use __init__ to give default values for some attributes.
Hint: Use default parameters: def __init__(self, x, y=0): self.x = x; self.y = y - y defaults to 0 if not provided.


15.7 Operator overloading
By defining special methods like __add__, __sub__, __mul__, etc., you can define how operators work on your objects. __add__ defines +, __mul__ defines *. This allows you to write natural code like p1 + p2 instead of p1.add(p2).

# Easy question (15.7): What special method allows you to define the behavior of +?

class Point:
    def __add__(self, other):  # This method is called ___
        return Point(self.x + other.x, self.y + other.y)

Hard question (15.7)
Describe how __add__ could be used to add two Point objects coordinate-wise.
Hint: def __add__(self, other): return Point(self.x + other.x, self.y + other.y). Then p3 = p1 + p2 works.

title: Python Programming Complete Quiz
text:
16. Classes and Objects

16.1 Creating a Point
You define a Point class and then instantiate it: p = Point(1, 2). The instance p is an object of type Point. Objects are created by calling the class like a function, which invokes __init__. Each instance has its own attributes.

# Easy question (16.1): What do we call a value like p created from a class?
# Answer: An ___

p = Point(1, 2)
# p is an ___

Hard question (16.1)
Describe what attributes a Point class might store and why.
Hint: A Point typically stores x and y coordinates to represent a location in 2D space. These attributes define the point’s position.


16.2 Creating a Line
A Line can be defined by two points or by coefficients of an equation (like y = mx + b). A line class might store two Point objects as endpoints, or store slope and intercept. The representation depends on what operations you need to perform.

# Easy question (16.2): What two pieces of information might you store in a Line defined by two points?

class Line:
    def __init__(self, p1, p2):
        self.p1 = ___  # first point
        self.p2 = ___  # second point

Hard question (16.2)
How could a Line class use two Point objects as attributes?
Hint: class Line: def __init__(self, p1, p2): self.start = p1; self.end = p2 - store Point objects as attributes.


16.3 Equivalence and identity
Equivalence (==) checks if objects have equal value (using __eq__). Identity (is) checks if they are the same object in memory. Two different objects can be equivalent but not identical. is compares memory addresses; == compares values (if __eq__ is defined).

# Easy question (16.3): Which operator checks identity: == or is?

p1 = Point(1, 2)
p2 = Point(1, 2)
result = p1 ___ p2  # use is or ==
print(result)  # Should print False (different objects)

Hard question (16.3)
Give a situation where two different objects compare equal with == but are not identical.
Hint: p1 = Point(1, 2) and p2 = Point(1, 2) are different objects (p1 is p2 is False) but equal (p1 == p2 is True if __eq__ is implemented).


16.4 Creating a Rectangle
A Rectangle might be defined by its position (corner point) and size (width and height), or by two corner points. Common attributes include x, y, width, height, or corner (a Point) plus width and height. The representation depends on your needs.

# Easy question (16.4): Name two attributes a Rectangle might have.

class Rectangle:
    def __init__(self):
        self.___ = 0  # attribute 1
        self.___ = 0  # attribute 2

Hard question (16.4)
How could a Rectangle store a Point for its corner in addition to width and height?
Hint: class Rectangle: def __init__(self, corner, width, height): self.corner = corner; self.width = width; self.height = height.


16.5 Changing rectangles
Since objects are mutable, you can change a rectangle’s position or size by modifying its attributes. You might move it by changing the corner point, or resize it by changing width/height. Methods like move(dx, dy) or resize(new_width, new_height) can encapsulate these operations.

# Easy question (16.5): How could you move a Rectangle to the right by 5 units?

rect.corner.x += ___  # fill in the amount

Hard question (16.5)
Explain why methods that modify objects are sometimes called “mutators”.
Hint: They mutate (change) the object’s state. Methods like move() or resize() modify the object’s attributes, changing its state.


16.6 Deep copy
A deep copy duplicates an object and all objects it refers to recursively. copy.deepcopy(obj) creates a completely independent copy. If an object contains nested mutable objects, deep copy ensures changes to nested objects in one copy don’t affect the other. This is important for complex nested structures.

# Easy question (16.6): What function from the copy module makes a deep copy?

import copy
rect_copy = copy.___(rect)  # fill in the function name

Hard question (16.6)
Why might changing a nested object in a shallow copy also change the original?
Hint: Shallow copy only copies the top level. If an object contains a list, both copies share the same list object. Modifying the list affects both.


16.7 Polymorphism
Polymorphism means different types can be used in the same way if they support the same operations. If different classes implement the same method name (like __str__ or draw()), functions can work with any of them. This allows writing generic code that works with multiple types.

# Easy question (16.7): Can different classes implement a method with the same name, like __str__?
# Answer: Yes/No

# Answer: ___

Hard question (16.7)
Explain how polymorphism allows functions to work with any sequence type (like list, tuple, or string).
Hint: All sequences support len(), indexing, and iteration. A function using these operations works with any sequence type without knowing the specific type.

title: Python Programming Complete Quiz
text:
17. Inheritance

17.1 Representing cards
A Card class can represent a playing card with a suit (hearts, diamonds, clubs, spades) and rank (Ace, 2-10, Jack, Queen, King). Cards are a good example for inheritance because different card games might need specialized card types that share basic card properties.

# Easy question (17.1): What two main attributes does a playing card usually have?

class Card:
    def __init__(self):
        self.___ = None  # attribute 1
        self.___ = None  # attribute 2

Hard question (17.1)
Why is a Card class a good candidate for use with inheritance?
Hint: Different card games might need specialized cards (e.g., UnoCard, PokerCard) that share basic properties (suit, rank) but have game-specific behavior.


17.2 Card attributes
Card attributes like suit and rank describe each card. Suits are typically strings ('hearts', 'diamonds', etc.) or numbers. Ranks can be strings ('Ace', 'King') or numbers (1-13). The representation affects how cards are compared and displayed.

# Easy question (17.2): How would you access the suit of a Card object c?

c = Card()
suit = c.___  # fill in how to access suit

Hard question (17.2)
Explain why it might be useful to store ranks as numbers instead of strings.
Hint: Numbers make comparison easier (rank1 < rank2), allow arithmetic operations, and can be mapped to display names when needed.


17.3 Printing cards
You can define __str__ to show a card nicely, like 'Ace of Spades' or 'A♠'. The __str__ method formats the card’s suit and rank into a readable string. This makes cards easy to display and debug. Different games might format cards differently.

# Easy question (17.3): What method controls how a Card prints?

class Card:
    def __str__(self):  # This method is called ___
        return f"{self.rank} of {self.suit}"

Hard question (17.3)
Describe how you would map numeric ranks to names like "Jack" or "Queen" for printing.
Hint: Use a dictionary: rank_names = {11: 'Jack', 12: 'Queen', 13: 'King', 1: 'Ace'} or use a list with index mapping.


17.4 Comparing cards
By defining comparison methods (__lt__, __eq__, etc.), you can compare cards by rank and suit. Cards might be compared by rank only, or by rank then suit. Comparison enables sorting decks and determining which card wins in games.

# Easy question (17.4): Why might you want to compare two cards?
# Answer: To determine which card is ___ or to sort them.

Hard question (17.4)
Explain a strategy to compare two cards using tuples of (rank, suit).
Hint: Convert cards to tuples (self.rank, self.suit) and compare those. Tuples compare lexicographically, so rank is compared first, then suit if ranks are equal.


17.5 Decks
A Deck class can represent a collection of 52 cards. It typically uses a list to store Card objects. The deck can be initialized with all 52 cards, shuffled, and cards can be dealt. A deck manages the collection of cards and provides operations on them.

# Easy question (17.5): What built-in Python collection type could you use inside Deck to store the cards?

class Deck:
    def __init__(self):
        self.cards = ___  # fill in the collection type

Hard question (17.5)
Describe how you might implement a method to deal one card from the deck.
Hint: Use self.cards.pop() to remove and return the last card, or self.cards.pop(0) for the first card. Check if deck is empty first.


17.6 Printing the deck
Deck can define __str__ to show all its cards. You might list them all, show a count, or format them nicely. The __str__ method should return a string representation, not print directly. This allows flexibility in how the deck is displayed.

# Easy question (17.6): Should __str__ return a string or print directly?
# Answer: It should ___

# Answer: return a string

Hard question (17.6)
Explain how you could use a loop and join to build a string listing all cards in the deck.
Hint: return ', '.join(str(card) for card in self.cards) - convert each card to string, then join with commas.


17.7 Add, remove, shuffle and sort
A Deck typically supports operations to add cards (append), remove cards (pop or remove), shuffle randomly (random.shuffle()), and sort (sort() with a key function). These operations modify the deck’s list of cards. Shuffling randomizes the order; sorting orders them by rank/suit.

# Easy question (17.7): What module provides a shuffle function?

import ___  # fill in the module name

Hard question (17.7)
Describe how inheritance might help you reuse list-like behavior inside a Deck class.
Hint: Deck could inherit from list to get list methods automatically, or use composition (a list as an attribute) and delegate methods to it.


17.8 Parents and children
In inheritance, a child class extends a parent (base) class, inheriting its attributes and methods. The child can add new methods, override parent methods, or call parent methods using super(). Inheritance enables code reuse and polymorphism. The parent class defines common behavior; children specialize it.

# Easy question (17.8): What function is commonly used to call a parent class method from a child class?

class Child(Parent):
    def method(self):
        ___.method(self)  # fill in the function name

Hard question (17.8)
Explain how inheritance can help avoid duplicating code across related classes.
Hint: Common code goes in the parent class. Children inherit it automatically and only add what’s unique. Changes to common behavior only need to be made in one place (the parent).

title: Bootcamp Introduction
text:
Schedule

Course 1: Python
Build strong Python programming foundations to apply and integrate ML/DL/AI in software systems.
Course 2: Applied Data Science
Calculate, analyze, visualize, interpret data and test hypothesis using Data Science.
Course 3: Applied Machine Learning
Build reliable predictive modeling pipelines, debug its issues, evaluate and compare alternatives.
Course 4: Applied Deep Learning
Use DL solution frameworks / libraries to apply AI to specific problems in vision and language.
Course 5: Agentic AI
Develop, debug, evaluate deploy, and monitor LLM-driven AI Agents to automate tasks involving unstructured data.
Course 6: Agentic Engineering
Use today’s best practices to accelerate software development in your codebase. Previously known as Vibe Coding.

title: Bootcamp Introduction
text:
Pedagogy







Figure: Benjamin Franklin quote

title: Bootcamp Introduction
text:
Learning Components

Slides
Lecture Notes
Labs
Exercises
Quizzes
Projects

title: Bootcamp Introduction
text:
1. Lecture Notes

The detailed learning material in written format.
Spend enough focused reading time to read them to completion.
When?: Read before (and after) class

title: Bootcamp Introduction
text:
2. Slides

Facilitate instructors’ lesson delivery.
Press S to open up Speaker Notes for more details.
When?: listten-in carefully to instructor in class.

title: Bootcamp Introduction
text:
3. Labs

Live documents of worked-out examples
“What if?” ask yourself, modify parts of the code cells and re-run them to verify understanding.
When?: to be done in class.

title: Bootcamp Introduction
text:
4. Exercises

Assignments with increasing level of difficulty
Some will test knowledge. Others will test skills (programming)
You are expected to finish them in class, and before the next day
When?: to be done in class.

title: Bootcamp Introduction
text:
Submit your Work
Your GitHub repo is your proof-of-work. Open the repo HassanAlgoz/B5 then:

Fork it from the GitHub website
Clone it using VS Code locally
Create a student/ folder in which you will include:

A student/README.md file with your full name in there
Exercises
Projects

title: Bootcamp Introduction
text:
Commits

First, git add and git commit the lab/exercise before solving it.
Each subsequent commit must have

Week number
Module number
Exercise number or name
Question number or title

title: Bootcamp Introduction
text:
Commit Examples

Execises:

"C1 M2 Ex1: init" (1st exercise)
"C1 M2 Ex1: q1" (question 1 solved)
"C1 M2 Ex1: q2"
"C1 M2 Ex2: init" (started 2nd exercise)

Projects:

"C1 Project: init"
"C1 Project: step 1. univariate analysis"
"C1 Project: step 2. pairplots"

title: Bootcamp Introduction
text:
Deadlines

Labs must be pushed daily (due end of tomorrow’s last session)
Exercises must be pushed by Thursday (due end of course)
Projects must be pushed by Sunday (due start of next course)
Commit history is analyzed to detect cheating
Zero points for grouping everything into one big commit

title: Bootcamp Introduction
text:
Evaluation

Weekly Quiz: test your understanding
Weekly Project (individual): apply learned skills
Project Presentation: on the day we start the next course (usually Sunday)
Prepare to be asked during the presentation about your work.

title: Bootcamp Introduction
text:
5. Quizzes
Test knowledge and skills based on learning outcomes.

Mostly questions from current course
Bonus: Some questions from previous courses
When?: Thursdays (end of course)

title: Bootcamp Introduction
text:
6. Projects
Apply learning to new situations.
Each project will be different depending on the course.

title: Bootcamp Introduction
text:
Presentation and Feedback
After completing the project, prepare a classroom Presentation on Sunday (before start of next course).

What goes into the presentation depends on the project
Completeness: points will be deducted if you missed requirements
Competency: points will be deducted if you lack understanding

you express ideas accurately in your own words (memorization + understanding)
your answer matches the level of detail required by the question (stay within topic)
you know when to say “I don’t know” (awareness of knowledge/skill gaps)

title: Bootcamp Introduction
text:
Why Presentations?

Your success in life depends on three key elements:

Your ability to speak
Your ability to write
The quality of your ideas

.. in that order (as Professor Winston puts it).
Your ability to communicate ideas is ultimately more critical to your success than the ideas themselves.

title: Bootcamp Introduction
text:
Learning and Desirable Difficulty
Your brain wants comfort; but as we all know- No pain. No gain. This is true for learning as it is true for exercising.

No one is perfect. Fear of mistakes is a psychological barrier to growth and exceeding your limits.
Make as much mistakes as you can; to get as much feedback as you can while you are in class.
Get feedback and check your assumptions and understanding; before you “move on”.
Getting help from others is good; but be sure YOU get it.

title: Bootcamp Introduction
text:
Policy: Getting Help from AI

Relying on AI to solve your issues is destructive to your learning.
Making mistakes is totally fine, but cheating will result in banning from the bootcamp.
We have sophisticated ways of detection.

title: Bootcamp Introduction
text:
Bad Students

Let AI handle everything
Use someone else’s work
Skip content and not put in any effort
Unaware of required work to be done
Don’t commit as per the instructions

title: Bootcamp Introduction
text:
Good Students

You are honest and consistent in putting in the required effort in class
You pay attention to instructions and ask when things aren’t clear
You ask the right questions; for the sake of learning
You are not afraid of making mistakes; for the sake of exceeding your limits

title: Shell Basics
text:
Why learn terminal commands?

Faster for repetitive tasks (rename/copy/search many files)
Works the same on local machine, servers, and cloud VMs
Easier to automate and document exact steps
Needed for many developer tools (git, python, package managers)
Useful when GUI is unavailable or too slow

title: Shell Basics
text:
When terminal beats GUI

You need to process many files at once
You want reproducible steps in notes/scripts
You work over SSH on a remote Linux machine
You debug environments and paths quickly
You chain commands into one workflow

title: Shell Basics
text:
Terminal command anatomy (POSIX style)
Most commands follow this pattern:
command [options] [required-args] [optional-args]
Examples:
ls -la /tmp
cp -r src_dir backup_dir
head -n 5 notes.txt

title: Shell Basics
text:
Required vs optional arguments

Required arguments: command fails without them

mv old.txt new.txt

Optional arguments: command still works without them

ls vs ls -la

Flags/options change behavior

-a, -l, -r, --help

title: Shell Basics
text:
Single-dash and double-dash flags

Short flags: -a, -l, -n 10
Long flags: --all, --help
Some commands allow combining short flags: ls -la
Always check exact syntax in docs (man command or command --help)

title: Shell Basics
text:
cd (change directory)

Purpose: move into another folder
macOS/Linux: cd, cd .., cd /path/to/dir
Windows cmd: cd, cd .., cd C:\path\to\dir
Common pattern:

pwd
cd ..
pwd

title: Shell Basics
text:
ls (list directory contents)

Purpose: show files/folders in current or target directory
macOS/Linux: ls, ls -la, ls /tmp
Windows cmd equivalent: dir
Useful flags:

-l long format
-a include hidden files

title: Shell Basics
text:
pwd (print working directory)

Purpose: show absolute path of current location
macOS/Linux: pwd
Windows cmd equivalent: cd (without args shows current path)

title: Shell Basics
text:
which (find executable path)

Purpose: show where a command is found in PATH
macOS/Linux: which python, which git
Windows cmd equivalent: where python
Common Pattern:

Program paths affect program behavior:

based on config files
based on nested files (like libraries)

Used alongside python --version and git --version to also know which version are we running

title: Shell Basics
text:
whoami (current user)

Purpose: show active username
macOS/Linux: whoami
Windows cmd equivalent: whoami
Used to know our access control scope:

Admin / Super-user has the highest level of privleges
others have different permissions

title: Shell Basics
text:
man (manual pages)

Purpose: open built-in docs for commands
macOS/Linux: man ls, man cp
Windows cmd equivalent: help (or command /?, e.g. dir /?)
Other ways to get help:

explainshell.com
tldr.sh
Your own notes

title: Shell Basics
text:
cat (print file contents)

Purpose: print file to terminal (and concatenate files)
macOS/Linux: cat file.txt, cat a.txt b.txt
Windows cmd equivalent: type file.txt

title: Shell Basics
text:
less (read large files page-by-page)

Purpose: scroll through long text safely
macOS/Linux: less big.log
Navigation: Space next page, b previous page, q quit
Windows cmd equivalent: more file.txt

title: Shell Basics
text:
touch (create empty file / update timestamp)

Purpose: create a file quickly if missing
macOS/Linux: touch notes.txt
Windows cmd equivalent: type nul > notes.txt (create empty file)

title: Shell Basics
text:
head (first lines of a file)

Purpose: preview beginning of a file
macOS/Linux: head file.txt, head -n 20 file.txt
Windows cmd equivalent: more +1 file.txt (rough equivalent)

title: Shell Basics
text:
tail (last lines of a file)

Purpose: preview end of a file
macOS/Linux: tail file.txt, tail -n 20 file.txt
Windows cmd equivalent: more -20 file.txt (or type file.txt | more, scroll to end)

title: Shell Basics
text:
mkdir (make directory)

Purpose: create directories
macOS/Linux: mkdir data, mkdir -p a/b/c
Windows cmd equivalent: mkdir data

title: Shell Basics
text:
file (identify file type)

Purpose: detect file content/type
macOS/Linux: file report.pdf, file script.sh
Windows cmd equivalent: no exact built-in direct equivalent in cmd

title: Shell Basics
text:
mv (move or rename)

Purpose: move file/folder or rename it
macOS/Linux: mv old.txt new.txt, mv file.txt docs/
Windows cmd equivalent: move (and ren for rename)

title: Shell Basics
text:
cp (copy files/folders)

Purpose: duplicate files/directories
macOS/Linux: cp a.txt b.txt, cp -r src_dir backup_dir
Windows cmd equivalent: copy (files), xcopy (folders)

title: Shell Basics
text:
find (search files by criteria)

Purpose: locate files/directories by name/type/pattern
macOS/Linux: find . -name "*.py", find . -type d
Windows cmd equivalent: dir /s /b *.py

title: Shell Basics
text:
cmp (compare two files byte-by-byte)

Purpose: quickly check if files differ
macOS/Linux: cmp file1.bin file2.bin
Windows cmd equivalent: fc /b file1.bin file2.bin

title: Shell Basics
text:
df (disk free space)

Purpose: show filesystem usage
macOS/Linux: df, df -h
Windows cmd equivalent: wmic logicaldisk get size,freespace,caption

title: Shell Basics
text:
Mini workflow example
pwd
mkdir -p demo/data
cd demo
touch notes.txt
cp notes.txt data/notes_copy.txt
ls -la data
df -h

title: Shell Basics
text:
Practice: classify command parts
For each command, identify command, flags, required args, optional args:
head -n 5 README.md
cp -r src_dir backup_dir
find . -name "*.qmd"

title: Shell Basics
text:
Opencode

Opencode is an AI coding assistant you can run from your shell
It works well for repo exploration, editing, and command execution workflows
Treat it like a pair-programmer: give clear goals, files, and constraints
Always review generated changes before committing

title: Shell Basics
text:
Opencode installation
Visit the website:
curl -fsSL https://opencode.ai/install | bash

title: Shell Basics
text:
Opencode quick start flow

Open your project in terminal
Start Opencode
Give a focused prompt with file paths and expected outcome
Review diffs, run tests, then commit

cd <project>
opencode

title: Shell Basics
text:
Prompting Opencode effectively

Be specific about:

target files (path/to/target/file)
required output (e.g., exercise questions)
constraints (keep style consistent, avoid breaking existing sections)

Ask for verification steps:

“run checks”
“show me the diff”
“summarize what changed”

title: Shell Basics
section: Security concerns with agents that run commands
text:
Security concerns with agents that run commands

Prompt injection: malicious instructions in files/URLs that trick the agent
Data exfiltration: agent can leak secrets (API keys, tokens, private data) via logs/output/network
Untrusted code execution: agent may suggest dangerous commands (delete files, download/run binaries)
Filesystem changes: agent can modify anything your user can access
Supply-chain risk: installing tooling from scripts/URLs may execute attacker-controlled code
Over-trusting generated output: agent can “hallucinate” commands or diffs that look correct but are wrong

title: Shell Basics
text:
Mitigations (what to do safely)

Never paste secrets into prompts; use env vars or secret stores when needed
Run as an unprivileged user; avoid sudo unless absolutely necessary
Review the diff first; only then run tests and accept changes
Prefer sandboxing: container/VM, restricted filesystem, and limited network access
Use least-privilege permissions and allowlisting where possible (e.g., only run known-safe commands)
Keep auditability: require “show commands” and capture logs before executing

title: Shell Basics
text:
Wrap-up

Terminal is about speed, repeatability, and control
You now know core navigation and file-management commands
You can read docs, pass flags/arguments, and inspect your system
