title: Introduction to Data Science
text:
Motivating story
NotebookLM comic (John Snow)
The invisible threat: London, 1854 — cholera in Soho. Consensus: miasma (bad air). John Snow asked for evidence: cholera might spread through contaminated water, not smell.

title: Introduction to Data Science
text:
Gathering the data
Snow could not see bacteria; he collected data instead:

Where each household lived.
Which drinking water they used.

He put deaths on a map — one mark per address — turning tables into spatial evidence.

title: Introduction to Data Science
text:
Identifying the pattern
Deaths clustered around the Broad Street pump, not evenly across “bad air.”

title: Introduction to Data Science
text:
Investigating anomalies
Strong hypotheses survive exceptions:

The brewery: Workers drank beer / a private well — not the pump.
The workhouse: Own well — almost no deaths amid surrounding cases.
The Hampstead widow: Died far from Soho; she still drank Broad Street water (and her niece, who visited).

Conclusion: the variable was water from that pump, not proximity to foul air.

title: Introduction to Data Science
text:
Data to action
Snow showed the board his map and reasoning; they acted on the evidence.

“I had an interview with the Board of Guardians of St. James’s parish… In consequence of what I said, the handle of the pump was removed on the following day.” — John Snow

The well was later found near a leaking cesspit.
(Snow 1855)

title: Introduction to Data Science
section: Why this matters for data science
text:
Why this matters for data science
John Snow’s workflow is still the template:

Question assumptions — demand evidence, not consensus alone.
Collect structured, verifiable information.
Visualize to see patterns lists hide.
Explain outliers — they often test the story.
Act — turn insight into a specific intervention.

Data science is using evidence to understand a situation and decide what to do — with modern tools for what Snow did on foot and on paper.

title: Introduction to Data Science
text:
What is data science?
Data (plural): digitally recorded pieces of information about events. Together they support a full picture — stored and processed on computers.

Numbers, text, images, sound, …
Types: numerical, categorical (and subtypes).
Different algorithms for different goals.


Data science is an interdisciplinary field that uses scientific methods, processes, algorithms, and systems to extract knowledge and insights from structured and unstructured data. It combines statistics, computer science, and domain knowledge to analyze data and support decisions and prediction.

title: Introduction to Data Science
text:
Three pillars

Domain knowledge — the right questions and interpreting results.
Math & statistics — sound conclusions from incomplete information.
Computer science — storage, algorithms, scale.

title: Introduction to Data Science
section: How did John Snow do it?
text:
How did John Snow do it?

Domain: physician who understood disease, the city, what variables to look at that may affect the outcome
Math & statistics: structured data, mapping, reasoning from pattern + exceptions.
Computer science: none — paper records and minimal computation.

Computers add speed and reach, not judgment. What we do with data still needs purpose and analysis.
In other words, computation and data are just means to an end. That end is: us gaining knowledge.

title: Introduction to Data Science
text:
Data science life cycle

Standard path from raw data to useful insight or product, and how roles map to the pipeline.

title: Introduction to Data Science
text:
The process

Collection — databases, logs, files, sensors, …
Cleaning — missing values, duplicates, formats, errors.
EDA — summaries and plots; trends, correlations, anomalies.
Model building — learn patterns / predict on new data.
Deployment — ship models into apps, APIs, or pipelines that run on new data.

title: Introduction to Data Science
text:
The roles
In large orgs these lines are sharp; in startups one person may wear several hats:

Data engineers — infrastructure: ingestion, storage, pipelines (collection & cleaning).
Data analysts — clean data, EDA, reports and dashboards (cleaning & EDA).
ML engineers — reliable, efficient models in production (build & deploy).
Data scientists — can span the whole cycle; often deepest in EDA and modeling, with enough engineering to get data and ship value.

See: SDAIA Professinoal Training in the Fields of Data and AI.

title: Introduction to Data Science
text:
Our World in Data
Want to look at the world through data? See many examples at: Our World in Data — research and data on global challenges.

title: Effective communication of exploratory results
text:
Why this matters
Graphs communicate ideas quickly—but images can also mislead.
This deck covers best practices for presenting data accurately and clearly. Visualization has room for creativity, yet solid principles still apply.

Open with the tension: a picture can be worth a thousand words, but it can also lie or mislead. The goal of this session is not to kill creativity—visualization is partly an art—but to give you guiding principles so your exploratory graphics present data accurately and effectively. Everything that follows supports that single aim.

title: Effective communication of exploratory results
text:
What’s wrong with this plot?


Pause on the image before you explain. Ask the room what feels off—scale, encoding, comparison, or story. You are priming them for the principles ahead: we will return to why bad choices happen (defaults, decoration, wrong chart type) as we go through each section. Keep this slide short; the next slides name the fixes.

title: Effective communication of exploratory results
text:
1. Keep it simple
Decide what you want the reader to see. Use color to group or differentiate levels meaningfully—not only to brighten the plot.
Pie slices are hard to compare; arbitrary colors distract; 3D rarely helps. A bar chart usually makes category magnitudes easier to read, especially with more than a few levels.


Use the classic manufacturing cost example mentally: five cost categories. On the pie, ask aloud whether your eye can really compare “Buildings and administration” to “Workplace materials”—slice angles are poor for magnitude. Call out three problems: hard comparisons, meaningless rainbow-like colors, and 3D that adds no insight. Then contrast with the bar chart: same categories, but length is easy to judge. Emphasize the rule: for more than a few categories, bars usually beat pies for reading values.

title: Effective communication of exploratory results
text:
2. Use color to draw attention
Add color on purpose: draw attention to what matters, and question defaults (e.g., do red and purple bars imply a relationship?).
Not everyone perceives color the same way—pair color with another cue (e.g., pattern) when you need to refer to highlighted elements in more than one way.


Beauty is fine, but defaults are a decision—often a bad one. Walk through (a) vs (b): in (b), color points to one message; in (a), redundant hues make viewers wonder if red and purple “mean” something related. Mention accessibility: color vision varies; panel (c) shows pairing color with pattern so you can refer to the highlighted category in two ways. This is a good place to say “if you only remember one thing about color: use it to direct attention, not to decorate.”

title: Effective communication of exploratory results
text:
3. Tell a story
Often the plot should include context that is not in the raw table—annotations, reference lines, or events that frame the narrative.
Example: mark the start of a fiscal year. A diagonal line (y = x) can separate points where one coordinate exceeds the other.


The dataset rarely contains the whole story. External information—policy dates, fiscal year starts, experimental conditions—belongs on the plot when it explains why the series looks the way it does. The text’s example: annotating the start of the university fiscal year. For scatterplots, a (y = x) line is standard: above the line, one variable exceeds the other; below, the reverse. That line is “context” encoded as geometry. Encourage students to ask: “What would a domain expert need to see that isn’t in the CSV?”

title: Effective communication of exploratory results
text:
4. Order matters
Software may order categories alphabetically by default—that is not always meaningful.
Consider ordering by magnitude or by the natural sequence of the question (e.g., survey response order). A misleading order can fool a quick scan of the axes.

Most tools alphabetize or use arbitrary order unless you override. Explain that alphabetical order is often meaningless for opinion or severity scales—readers skim the axis and infer a trend that isn’t there. Offer two better options: sort by count (tallest to shortest) when the story is “who is biggest,” and survey or logical order when the scale itself is ordinal (e.g., “very well” through “very badly”). Stress: wrong order misleads even when the numbers are correct. The next slide gives a real survey where question order is the right order.

title: Effective communication of exploratory results
text:
Brexit opinion (YouGov, Sept 2019)
Survey of 1,639 adults in Great Britain: How well or badly do you think the government are doing at handling Britain’s exit from the European Union?

Very well

Fairly well

Fairly badly

Very badly

Don’t know



YouGov, September 2019, n = 1,639 Great Britain adults—link is on the slide for anyone who wants the PDF. The question wording matters; the response options have a natural order from positive to negative (plus “Don’t know”). That is why alphabetical bar order fails here: the right order follows how the question was asked, as in the figure. Connect back to the previous slide: this is the “Figure 6.4” story in the book—(a) alphabetical, (b) by height, (c) by survey order. Walk the audience through what each ordering emphasizes before they stare at viz_4.

title: Effective communication of exploratory results
text:
5. Make labels easy to read
Same numbers, different layout: horizontal bars often leave more room for category labels than vertical bars.
Clear labels → more learning from the chart. Aim for succinct, readable text.


This continues the Brexit theme: results by region in stacked bars. (a) vertical vs (b) horizontal—the values are identical; only layout changes. Horizontal bars buy space for long region names on the axis. Repeat the mantra: the easier the labels, the more the reader actually reads. If something is hard to read, they skip it and your chart under-delivers. Tie to the chapter goal: maximum information, clearly, not maximum ink.

title: Effective communication of exploratory results
text:
6. Pick a purpose
Every graphical choice should serve the research question. Defaults are not always the right story.
Stacked bars emphasize one kind of comparison; faceting or alternative encodings may fit another. Use informative labels, titles, and sources when you can.


Stacked bars answer “what is the composition within each group?”—not always the question you care about. Same data, three stories: (a) if proportions across regions are the headline, stacked or normalized views help. (b) if counts matter—not just shares—show totals or axes that preserve magnitude. (c) faceting—one small multiple per region—when you want readers to compare distributions within region without stacking confusion. Mention that good figures also carry titles and a data source URL; the book highlights that on (c). Preview the next slide: more variants of the same idea.

title: Effective communication of exploratory results
text:
Same data, different emphasis


Briefly contrast this panel with the previous: reinforce that encoding choice = editorial choice. Ask: “For your analysis, is the story about share, about total (N), or about per-region detail?” There is no single “correct” chart—only a chart that matches the question. Remind them to strip redundant ink and to label what matters.

title: Effective communication of exploratory results
text:
7. Select meaningful colors
Avoid rainbow defaults when they do not match your variable. Choose palettes that are distinct and work for color-vision diversity (e.g., cividis for ordinal data—Nuñez, Anderton, and Renslow 2018).


Rainbow scales are popular in defaults but often perceptually uneven—equal steps in data don’t look equal in hue. Research-backed palettes (the slide mentions cividis for ordinal data) aim for discriminability and robustness across common forms of color-vision deficiency. Same plot, two themes: let the audience see how “prettier” is not the same as “more informative.” Point to the paper if someone teaches or publishes in this space.

title: Effective communication of exploratory results
text:
Takeaway
Your graph should carry maximum information in minimum ink: clear labels (often horizontal), strong titles, and data source when possible.

One-sentence synthesis of the chapter: maximal information, succinctly and clearly—Tufte’s spirit without naming him if you prefer. Horizontal labels when you can, titles that state the claim, and sources so the figure is trustworthy. This is a good moment to loop back to the opening “bad plot”: ask which principles it violated. End upbeat: these habits become automatic with practice.

title: Effective communication of exploratory results
text:
Quantitative perception
How do we perceive quantities from visual properties of a chart?


Transition from “what to draw” to “how humans read marks.” The perception figure summarizes which encodings we judge well (position along a common scale) vs poorly (area, angle, hue without order). Use this slide to justify why bar length beats pie angle, and why careful color matters. Keep it high level here; the next slide sends them to deeper resources.

title: Effective communication of exploratory results
text:
More on perception and encoding

Slide deck: Data Visualization Best Practices — Catherine Mulbrandon

Plot building blocks: Plot properties — Seaborn (color, style, size, text)


Mulbrandon’s deck is an excellent follow-up for economics-style communication. Seaborn’s “Plot properties” tutorial is practical for implementation: how library defaults map to color, style, size, and text—good for lab work after this lecture. Encourage bookmarking rather than reading live.

title: Effective communication of exploratory results
text:
Color scheme tools

ColorBrewer 2.0

Coolors


ColorBrewer is the classic tool for map- and chart-ready palettes with accessibility in mind. Coolors helps when you need to explore combinations quickly—still check contrast and color-blind simulation before finalizing. These are for after you know your data type (qualitative vs sequential vs diverging).

title: Effective communication of exploratory results
text:
References

OpenIntro: Introduction to Modern Statistics

CS109A | Harvard — Visualization lecture


Credit the OpenIntro material this chapter draws from, and Harvard CS109A for a full lecture treatment of visualization if students want alternate explanations and examples. Close the session; invite questions.

title: Hypothesis Testing
text:
From description to claims
Descriptive statistics summarizes what you measured.
Inferential statistics goes further: informed guesses about a population, and checking claims against data—using clear rules.

Frame the notebook’s arc: we already know how to summarize samples; now we ask whether patterns are more than noise. Tie to the course story (e.g., “Are NBA players really taller?”): that is inference, not just plotting means.

title: Hypothesis Testing
text:
Descriptive vs inferential



Describe: means, plots, tables for the data in hand.

Infer: use those summaries to reason about what we did not observe—under uncertainty.

We make claims and test them with defined procedures.




Walk the diagram: descriptive stays with the sample; inference reaches toward the population or toward “if we repeated the study.” The external image in the notebook serves the same role; this local asset matches the idea. Emphasize: inference always involves assumptions and limits—we will name a few (normality, independence) later.

title: Hypothesis Testing
text:
Everyday claims
People assert things like:

Coffee keeps you awake at night.

Exercise makes you smarter.

“Everyone” is addicted to smartphones.

Question: how do we test such statements against reality—not with one story, but with data?

Use this slide to surface anecdotes vs systematic evidence. Do not dismiss anecdotes; they generate hypotheses—but they do not replace sampling and tests. Transition: we formalize claims as (H_0) and (H_a).

title: Hypothesis Testing
text:
Two competing statements
In every formal test we line up:

Null hypothesis ((H_0)): the “status quo” or no effect. Any difference you see could be chance (sampling noise).

Alternative hypothesis ((H_a) or (H_1)): what you seek evidence for—a real effect, difference, or relationship.


Stress language: we never “prove (H_0)” in a single study; we fail to reject it when evidence is weak. That sets up the p-value slide. Students often confuse “accept null” with “no effect”—correct that gently.

title: Hypothesis Testing
text:
Why anecdotes fail
Anecdotal evidence: personal stories or isolated incidents—not a systematic sample.
We turn anecdotes into parameters and data:

Swordfish mercury → compare mean mercury in many fish to a regulatory threshold.

Duke graduation time → compare Duke’s distribution to a national benchmark.

A new drug → compare survival in treatment vs control (placebo), not one patient.


Use the notebook’s three vignettes quickly: news story, two students at Duke, friend’s dad. For each, name the population parameter (mean (), proportion (p), survival rate) and the need for comparison groups where relevant.

title: Hypothesis Testing
text:
Example formulations









Example
(H_0)
(H_a)
Parameter




Mercury
Mean () 1.0 ppm
Mean (>) 1.0 ppm
()


Duke grad
Rate () national avg.
Rate (>) national avg.
(p)


Heart drug
Death rate (drug) (=) (placebo)
Death rate (drug) (<) (placebo)
survival / (p)




This table is the bridge from English claims to symbols. Pause on direction: one-sided vs two-sided tests come from how (H_a) is stated. The lab will mirror this when they write hypotheses for their own questions.

title: Hypothesis Testing
text:
Which test answers your question?
Methods differ by what you ask:

Difference in one numeric variable (often t-tests).

Association between two categorical variables (chi-square).

Association between two numeric variables (Pearson correlation—and alternatives).


Keep it high level: this deck focuses on t-tests; chi-square and Pearson are pointers to later work or other modules. The decision tree slide will show how these pieces fit on one map.

title: Hypothesis Testing
text:
p-values and ()
Test output includes a p-value.

If p (< ) (convention: (= 0.05)), we reject (H_0).

If p (), we fail to reject (H_0)—not the same as “(H_0) is true.”

Courtroom analogy: (H_0) () “innocent until proven guilty.” “Not guilty” can mean lack of evidence, not proof of innocence.

Hammer the wording: “fail to reject” not “accept”. Same as the notebook. If time, mention that () is a community convention, not a law of nature—preregistered studies may justify other thresholds.

title: Hypothesis Testing
text:
Testing differences: three t-test situations


t-tests compare means in three setups:

One sample: sample mean vs a reference (population or standard).

Independent samples: two unrelated groups (e.g., treatment vs control).

Paired: same units twice, or natural pairs (twins, matched pairs).

Video overview (numiqo).




Let the figure carry structure. After this slide, unpack each case in one slide each—fast, because the picture is the anchor.

title: Hypothesis Testing
section: Case 1: One sample vs a reference
text:
Case 1: One sample vs a reference
Compare ({x}) from your sample to a known or assumed ():

Student heights vs national average.

IQ in a group vs population mean.

Cholesterol vs clinical guideline.

Notebook example: NBA player heights vs general population ( ttest_1samp ).

“Population” here often means a benchmark from literature or policy, not a full census. The NBA vs humans example is vivid: huge separation, tiny p-value—good for pedagogy, not subtle statistics.

title: Hypothesis Testing
text:
Case 2: Two independent samples
Two different groups:

Control (placebo) vs treatment (drug).

Male vs female penguins on body mass (ttest_ind).

Notebook: tips from smokers vs non-smokers (same function, different story).

Check independence: different rows, no pairing. Common mistake: using ttest_ind on before/after data from the same people—that is paired.

title: Hypothesis Testing
text:
Case 3: Paired data
3.1 One group, two measurements: before/after, method A vs method B on the same subjects (spinach and grip strength in the notebook).
3.2 Natural pairs: twin A vs twin B, husband–wife, mother–baby—each row is a pair (ttest_rel).

Contrast with independent: pairing removes between-person noise and can hugely change the result. The notebook’s grip-strength example is ideal for code walkthrough in the notebook itself.

title: Hypothesis Testing
text:
SciPy: functions to remember



Idea
SciPy




One sample vs mean
ttest_1samp


Two independent groups
ttest_ind


Paired differences
ttest_rel


Numeric association
pearsonr, spearmanr


Categorical association
chi2_contingency (+ association)




Point to the SciPy stats docs—students will copy-paste the right function once the question type is clear. Mention alternative (two-sided, less, greater) matches how (H_a) is directional.

title: Hypothesis Testing
text:
Choosing a test (decision tree)



Comparison or relationship?

How many groups? Paired or not?

Variable types: numeric vs categorical?

Use the tree to narrow choices before coding.




This is the same figure as the notebook. Encourage students to print or bookmark it. It prevents “I ran a t-test because I always run a t-test.”

title: Hypothesis Testing
text:
Assumptions (and Plan B)
Classical t-tests assume roughly normal data (or large enough (n) for the mean to behave).
If data are heavily skewed or dominated by outliers, non-parametric tests (e.g., Mann–Whitney, Wilcoxon) use ranks and are often safer for reporting “location” differences honestly.

Do not turn this into a full assumptions course—flag check plots (histogram, QQ) and robust alternatives. The notebook states this in one paragraph; match that tone.

title: Hypothesis Testing
text:
Beyond two groups: ANOVA (preview)
A t-test compares two means. With three or more groups (e.g., penguin species on body mass), use one-way ANOVA as the omnibus test; Tukey HSD (or similar) for pairwise comparisons—not many separate t-tests without correction (inflates false positives).

The notebook adds ANOVA/Tukey briefly; mention multiple comparisons problem in one sentence. Full treatment belongs to follow-up reading or another session.

title: Hypothesis testing
text:
Where to go next

Lesson notebook: 01_hypothesis_testing.ipynb — NBA heights, penguins, tips, paired spinach, ANOVA.

SciPy reference: stats.


Close the loop: slides = ideas and map; notebook = code, plots, numbers. Assign or suggest working through one example end-to-end before the next class.

title: Numerical correlation
text:
What correlation summarizes
Correlation measures how two numeric variables co-vary: when one tends to be high or low, does the other tend to follow?
We start with the picture—a scatterplot—and then attach a single number: the Pearson correlation coefficient (r).

Position this lesson after hypothesis tests on means: correlation is about association between continuous measurements, not difference of group means.

title: Numerical correlation
text:
Visual working definition


Graphically, correlation reflects how tightly points cluster around a straight line.

Perfect linearity → (r = ).

Strong trends → (r) near ().

Little linear structure → (r) near (0).





Walk the classic eight-panel figure (often called “Figure 7.10” in texts). Stress linear: a beautiful curve can still yield (r ). That tees up the “nonlinear” and “linear only” slides later.

title: Numerical correlation
text:
Example: hybrid cars
The hybrid table (adapted from Larry Winner’s archive) lists U.S. hybrid passenger cars (1997–2013).
Variables: vehicle, year, msrp, acceleration, mpg, class.
The notebook plots scatterplots and then computes (r)—same workflow students should copy.

Keep this light: one sentence on provenance builds trust. Send them to the notebook for the full table and code (path_data + 'hybrid.csv').

title: Numerical correlation
text:
Positive association: price vs acceleration
Scatter: msrp (vertical) vs acceleration (horizontal).
Points slope upward: faster acceleration tends to go with higher price on average—positive association.

Match the notebook’s “Relationship 1.” Mention the typo in the notebook (“accelaration”) only if someone asks—don’t derail.

title: Numerical correlation
text:
Negative association: price vs efficiency
Scatter: msrp vs mpg.
Points slope downward: higher mpg tends to pair with lower price on average—negative association.
The notebook also notes confounding: quick cars are often less fuel-efficient and pricier, so stories intertwine.

“Relationship 2” in the notebook. Good moment to say correlation does not assign which variable “causes” which—only joint behavior.

title: Numerical correlation
text:
Nonlinear pattern
The price–mpg cloud often follows a curve, not a straight line.
Pearson (r) still measures linear fit; a strong curved relationship may produce a modest (r) if the curve isn’t well approximated by a line.


Use plots to see curvature. For monotonic but nonlinear relationships, Spearman rank correlation (in the notebook / SciPy) is a common companion to Pearson.




The notebook explicitly says points cluster around a curve, not a line. Tie to spearmanr when you demo code.

title: Numerical correlation
text:
The Pearson correlation coefficient
Idea: standardized cross-products / average “co-movement” after centering and scaling.
In code: pandas .corr() on two columns, or scipy.stats.pearsonr (returns (r) and a p-value for a test of “no linear association”).
The notebook breaks down the calculation step-by-step—use it when teaching the algebra.

Do not derive the full sum on slides unless your audience loves algebra; point to the “Calculation breakdown” section. Emphasize pairwise complete observations when there are missing values.

title: Numerical correlation
text:
Correlation is unitless
Standardizing removes units: (r) is comparable across studies (e.g., dollars vs inches doesn’t matter).


Same underlying relationship, different scales on the axes—same (r).
This is why (r) is useful for comparing strength across different variable pairs.




Walk the figure: rescaling axes stretches the plot but not the linear association strength. Connect to z-scores if your class already knows them.

title: Numerical correlation
text:
Association () causation
A correlation is not a mechanism. Confounders, reverse causation, and selection can produce strong (r) with no direct causal link.
Examples (from the notebook):

Weight and math ability (age confounding).

Chocolate and Nobel prizes (country-level quirks).

Liberal arts bachelor’s degrees and pediatricians (spurious time-series overlap).



Famous chocolate vs Nobel plot: entertaining, not a dietary policy.
NEJM paper — discuss ecological / country-level aggregation.




Keep tone curious, not preachy. Ask: “What’s a confounder here?” (wealth, health care, R&D spending, …). Good bridge to study design.

title: Numerical correlation
text:
Another spurious series


Two trends can look correlated over time even when unrelated—always ask who is the unit of analysis and what drives each series.




“Liberal arts bachelor’s vs pediatricians” is the notebook’s third bullet; this figure illustrates nonsense coupling generically. Reinforce: causality needs design (RCT, natural experiments), not just (r).

title: Numerical correlation
text:
Toward causality
Correlation supports prediction and hypothesis generation; causal claims need design: randomization, control groups, careful measurement, and domain theory.
Deeper read: Study design — Introduction to Modern Statistics (Çetinkaya-Rundel & Hardin).

One minute max. Students who want “causal inference” as a next course get a breadcrumb.

title: Numerical correlation
text:
Correlation measures linear association
Pearson (r) answers: “How well does a straight line summarize the cloud?”
If the truth is nonlinear (U-shaped, logistic, etc.), (r) can understate a strong relationship—or misstate it.
Supplement: Spearman (rank) for monotonic trends; visualize first.

Optional demo: parabola-shaped data with (r ). The correlation-non-linear asset already supports the narrative.

title: Numerical correlation
text:
Correlation is sensitive to outliers
A few extreme points can pull the best-fit line and change (r) a lot.
Practice: plot residuals, consider robust methods or winsorizing after investigation—not before hiding real structure.

The notebook shows before/after outlier scenarios. Live session: drag one point in a tool or show their plots. Mention influence vs leverage only in advanced sections.

title: Numerical correlation
text:
Ecological correlation
Correlations computed on aggregates (states, countries) can be stronger than correlations among individuals—different population, different story.
Example: Critical Reading vs Math SAT by state (notebook): interesting for policy, not the same as student-level association.

Classic warning: ecological fallacy—inferring individual behavior from group summaries. Good for discussions of election maps and health data.

title: Numerical correlation
text:
Application and code

Notebook: 03_num_correlation.ipynb — hybrid plots, manual decomposition, pearsonr, Spearman, ecological SAT example.

Playground: Inferential Thinking — correlation (referenced in the notebook).


Close with: “Scatter first, number second, causal language last.” Assign one pair of variables from their own project and require a plot and (r).

title: Introduction to Data Science
text:
Link to comic


In the late summer of 1854, the Soho district of London experienced a devastating outbreak of cholera. Within a few days, hundreds of people died. At the time, the prevailing medical consensus was the “miasma theory”—the idea that diseases were spread by foul-smelling, poisoned air emanating from decaying organic matter.
Because Soho was crowded and had poor sanitation, the miasma theory seemed to fit. The government’s public health interventions focused on trying to clean up the smell. However, one physician, John Snow, approached the problem differently. He did not accept the established consensus because it lacked rigorous empirical support. He suspected cholera was caused by something ingested, specifically through contaminated water.



Snow could not see the cholera bacteria; the microscope technology of the time was not advanced enough to easily identify it, and the germ theory of disease was not yet established. Instead of arguing theory, Snow gathered data.
He walked the streets of Soho, knocking on doors and interviewing the families of those who had died. He recorded two critical pieces of information for every household:

The location of the home.
The source of their drinking water.

He then took this tabular data and visualized it. He took a street map of Soho and drew a black bar for every cholera death at the exact address where it occurred.



When the data was plotted spatially, a clear pattern emerged. The deaths were not spread evenly through the “bad air” of Soho. They were heavily concentrated in a specific cluster around the public water pump on Broad Street.



Death Counts Mapped in the neighborhood


In data analysis, identifying a trend is only the first step; a robust model must also account for outliers and anomalies. Snow looked closely at the exceptions to his spatial pattern:

The Brewery: There was a large brewery located very close to the Broad Street pump, yet none of the workers had contracted cholera. Snow investigated and found that the workers were given a daily allowance of beer and rarely drank from the public pump. Furthermore, the brewery had its own private deep well.
The Workhouse: A nearby workhouse surrounded by cholera cases had almost no deaths. Snow found they also had their own private well.
The Hampstead Widow: Snow found a record of a widow who died of cholera miles away in Hampstead, an area entirely free of the outbreak. He visited her home and discovered that she used to live in Soho and liked the taste of the Broad Street water so much that she had a servant fetch a large bottle of it every week. Her niece, who visited and drank the water, also died.

These anomalies, rather than breaking his hypothesis, provided the strongest evidence for it. The variable controlling the disease was not proximity to bad air, but the specific consumption of water from the Broad Street pump.



Snow presented his map and his findings to the local Board of Guardians. The evidence was clear enough to prompt an immediate, physical intervention.

“I had an interview with the Board of Guardians of St. James’s parish… In consequence of what I said, the handle of the pump was removed on the following day.” — John Snow

The removal of the pump handle stopped the immediate outbreak. Later investigations revealed that the Broad Street well had been dug mere feet from a leaking cesspit.

[@snow1855]



John Snow’s work perfectly encapsulates the core process of data science, long before computers existed:

Questioning Assumptions: He did not accept the “miasma” consensus because it was not supported by evidence.
Data Collection: He systematically gathered raw, verifiable information (locations and water sources).
Visualization: He mapped the data to reveal spatial patterns that were invisible in a simple list of numbers.
Investigating Outliers: He rigorously checked his hypothesis against the exceptions (the brewery, the widow).
Actionable Insight: He translated his findings into a specific, targeted intervention (removing the pump handle) that solved the problem.

Data Science is not merely about writing code or knowing complex equations. It is about using evidence to uncover the truth of a situation and making informed decisions. Throughout this course, you will learn the modern tools to do exactly what John Snow did: gather data, find the pattern, explain the anomalies, and remove the pump handle.




Data (plural) is a digitally recorded small piece of information about an event. When put together, one can piece together the full picture. But, first, we store them on computers.

Data can be numbers, text, images, sounds, etc.
Data also falls into types: numerical, categorical and sub-types.
Data is analyzed using different algorithms for different purposes.

.. there is alot to say about data .. hence, we have Data Science.

Data Science is an interdisciplinary field that uses scientific methods, processes, algorithms, and systems to extract knowledge and insights from structured and unstructured data. It combines principles and techniques from statistics, computer science, and domain-specific knowledge to analyze, interpret, and leverage data for decision-making and predictive analytics.


Domain Knowledge is essential for asking the right questions and for understanding the answers produced by computational tools.
Math & Statistics studies how to make robust conclusions based on incomplete information.
Computer Science is needed since data are stored on computers and processed by algorithms.


Image Source: https://www.researchgate.net/figure/Data-Science-Venn-Diagram_fig1_365946272



Domain Knowledge: Snow is a doctor
Math & Statistics: Snow used data, plotted it, and drew conclusions
Computer Science: Snow did not have computers, but the data was stored on paper, and he did not need to do much computations.

The use of computers does not give us more intelligence, it simply gives us the ability to get data in real-time from users of our systems. What we do with this data needs our own analytical mindset and purpose.





This image illustrates the standard Data Science Life Cycle (the sequence of steps required to turn raw data into useful tools or insights) and maps those steps to the specific job titles responsible for them.


This represents the sequential journey of data from its raw state to a finished product.

Collection: This is the act of gathering raw data from various sources. It involves extracting records from databases, logging user actions on a website, or downloading files.
Cleaning: Raw data is rarely ready to use. It contains missing values, duplicates, and errors. Cleaning is the process of standardizing formats and fixing or removing corrupted data so that it doesn’t skew the final results.
Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA): Before building complex systems, you must understand what the data looks like. This involves calculating basic statistics (averages, ranges) and creating visual charts to find trends, correlations, or anomalies. 4. Model Building: This is where machine learning happens. It involves feeding the cleaned, understood data into mathematical algorithms so the computer can learn to recognize patterns and make predictions on new, unseen data.
Model Deployment: A predictive model is useless if it only sits on a researcher’s laptop. Deployment is the software engineering process of integrating that model into a live application, website, or server where it can process new data in real-time.




Different specialists focus on different parts of the pipeline.

Data Engineers (Collection & Cleaning): These are essentially software engineers who build the underlying infrastructure. They construct the databases and automated pipelines that safely extract, store, and organize massive amounts of data so that others can use it.
Data Analysts (Cleaning & EDA): Analysts focus on understanding historical data to answer specific business questions. They take the data provided by the engineers, ensure it is clean for their specific needs, and build reports or dashboards to explain what has happened in the past.
Machine Learning Engineers (Model Building & Deployment): These professionals bridge the gap between mathematics and software engineering. They take a predictive concept and write the code to ensure it runs efficiently, accurately, and reliably in a live production environment.
Data Scientists (The Entire Pipeline): Data Scientists are the generalists of the field. As the long bottom bar shows, they possess the technical skills to handle the entire lifecycle from start to finish. In practice, they primarily focus heavily on EDA and Model Building, but they must understand data engineering to get their data, and deployment to ensure their models are usable.

While this diagram cleanly separates these roles, the real world is often messier. In large technology companies, these divisions are strict. In smaller companies or startups, a single “Data Scientist” might be expected to do the jobs of all four roles simultaneously.




OWID - Research and data to make progress against the world’s largest problems.

Data Scientists (The Entire Pipeline): Data Scientists are the generalists of the field. As the long bottom bar shows, they possess the technical skills to handle the entire lifecycle from start to finish. In practice, they primarily focus heavily on EDA and Model Building, but they must understand data engineering to get their data, and deployment to ensure their models are usable.

While this diagram cleanly separates these roles, the real world is often messier. In large technology companies, these divisions are strict. In smaller companies or startups, a single “Data Scientist” might be expected to do the jobs of all four roles simultaneously.

title: Effective communication of exploratory results
text:
Graphs can powerfully communicate ideas directly and quickly. We all know, after all, that “a picture is worth 1000 words.” Unfortunately, however, there are times when an image conveys a message which is inaccurate or misleading.
This chapter focuses on how graphs can best be utilized to present data accurately and effectively. Along with data modeling, creative visualization is somewhat of an art. However, even with an art, there are recommended guiding principles. We provide a few best practices for creating data visualizations.






When creating a graphic, keep in mind what it is that you’d like your reader to see. Colors should be used to group items or differentiate levels in meaningful ways. Colors can be distracting when they are only used to brighten up the plot.
Consider a manufacturing company that has summarized its costs into five different categories. In the two graphics provided in Figure 6.1, notice that the magnitudes in the pie chart in Figure 6.1 (a) are difficult for the eye to compare. That is, can your eye tell how different “Buildings and administration” is from “Workplace materials” when looking at the slices of pie? Additionally, the colors in the pie chart do not mean anything and are therefore distracting. Lastly, the three-dimensional aspect of the image does not improve the reader’s ability to understand the data presented.
As an alternative, a bar plot is been provided in Figure 6.1 (b). Notice how much easier it is to identify the magnitude of the differences across categories while not being distracted by other aspects of the image. Typically, a bar plot will be easier for the reader to digest than a pie chart, especially if the categorical data being plotted has more than just a few levels.




There are many reasons why you might choose to add color to your plots. An important principle to keep in mind is to use color to draw attention. Of course, you should still think about how visually pleasing your visualization is, and if you’re adding color for making it visually pleasing without drawing attention to a particular feature, that might be fine. However, you should be critical of default coloring and explicitly decide whether to include color and how. Notice that in Figure 6.2 (b) the coloring is done in such a way to draw the reader’s attention to one particular piece of information. The default coloring in Figure 6.2 (a) can be distracting and makes the reader question, for example, is there something similar about the red and purple bars? Also note that not everyone sees color the same way, often it’s useful to add color and one more feature (e.g., pattern) so that you can refer to the features you’re drawing attention to in multiple ways, as shown in Figure 6.2 (c). 



For many graphs, an important aspect is the inclusion of information which is not provided in the dataset that is being plotted. The external information serves to contextualize the data and helps communicate the narrative of the research.
In Figure 6.3, the graph on the right is annotated with information about the start of the university’s fiscal year which contextualizes the information provided by the data. Sometimes the additional information may be a diagonal line given by \(y = x\), points above the line quickly show the reader which values have a coordinate larger than the coordinate; points below the line show the opposite. 



Most software programs have built in methods for some of the plot details – some order levels alphabetically, some provide functionality for arranging them in a custom order. As seen in Figure 6.4 (a), the alphabetical ordering isn’t particularly meaningful for describing the data. Sometimes it makes sense to order the bars from tallest to shortest (or vice versa), as shown in Figure 6.4 (b). But in this case, the best ordering is probably the one in which the questions were asked, as shown in Figure 6.4 (c). An ordering which does not make sense in the context of the problem (e.g., alphabetically here), can mislead the reader who might take a quick glance at the axes and not read the bar labels carefully.
In September 2019, YouGov survey asked 1,639 Great Britain adults the following question:
How well or badly do you think the government are doing at handling Britain’s exit from the European Union?

Very well
Fairly well
Fairly badly
Very badly
Don’t know 




The Brexit survey results were additionally broken down by region in Great Britain. The stacked bar plot allows for comparison of Brexit opinion across the five regions. In Figure 6.5 (a) the bars are vertical and in Figure 6.5 (b) they are horizontal. While the quantitative information in the two graphics is identical, flipping the graph and creating horizontal bars provides more space for the axis labels. The easier the categories are to read, the more the reader will learn from the visualization. Remember, the goal is to convey as much information as possible in a succinct and clear manner. 



Every graphical decision should be made with a purpose. As previously mentioned, sticking with default options is not always best for conveying the narrative of your data story. Stacked bar plots tell one part of a story. Depending on your research question, they may not tell the part of the story most important to the research.
Figure 6.6 provides three different ways of representing the same information. If the most important comparison across regions is proportion, you might prefer Figure 6.6 (a). If the most important comparison across regions also considers the total number of individuals in the region, you might prefer Figure 6.6 (b). If a separate bar plot for each region makes the point you’d like, use Figure 6.6 (c), which has been faceted by region. Figure 6.6 (c) also provides full titles and a succinct URL with the data source. Other deliberate decisions to consider include using informative labels and avoiding redundancy. 




One last consideration for building graphs is to consider color choices. Default or rainbow colors are not always the choice which will best distinguish the level of your variables. Much research has been done to find color combinations which are distinct and which are clear for differently sighted individuals. The cividis scale works well with ordinal data. (Nuñez, Anderton, and Renslow 2018) Figure 6.7 shows the same plot with two different color themes.  In this chapter different representations are contrasted to demonstrate best practices in creating graphs. The fundamental principle is that your graph should provide maximal information succinctly and clearly. Labels should be clear and oriented horizontally for the reader. Don’t forget titles and, if possible, include the source of the data.



How do we percieve quantities as given by the plot properties?  Checkout the slide deck: Data Visualization Best Practices by Catherine Mulbrandon.
For more details on properties of: color, style, size, text, checkout the Plot Properties | Seaborn




http://colorbrewer2.org
https://coolors.co/

References:

Open Intro: Introduction to Modern Statistics
CS109A | Harvard

title: Statistics
text:
Motivation
Question: are NBA Players really taller than most people?

title: Statistics
text:
Population
Population (مجتمع إحصائي): The object which the research intends to generalize its results towards.

People: like every person living in a specific neighborhood
Things: like every lightbulb produced in a factory today

Sample (عينة): is subset of Population. Our sample is NBA Players (at some point in time).

title: Statistics
section: Thinking in levels about population and sample
text:
Thinking in levels about population and sample
In reality, we don’t always have access to all population. Hence, it is helpful to think in levels, The smallest of which is the Subject:


In research, these terms describe a “funnel” that narrows down from a massive group of people to the specific individuals being studied.

Population: The entire broad group of people that your research results are intended to describe (e.g., “all adults with Type 2 diabetes”).
Target Population: The specific subset of the population that meets your formal “eligibility rules” or sampling criteria (e.g., “adults with Type 2 diabetes living in Canada, aged 40–60”).
Accessible Population: The actual portion of the target population that the researcher can realistically reach and recruit from (e.g., “adults with Type 2 diabetes at three specific clinics in Toronto”).
Sample: The small group of individuals chosen from the accessible population who actually take part in the study.
Subject, Participant, or Informant: A single individual within the sample.

title: Statistics
text:
Variables
Variables (متغيرات) are the shared attributes of the subjects. They may be:

Numerical (عددي) weight, temperature, or duration.
Categorical (فئوي) “Success/Failure,” color, or brand.

Relevance is key. Pick attributes that you think might explian logical relationship to the conclusions you want to make.
In our case, we are looking at the height variable.

title: Statistics
text:
Statistics
Statistics (إحصاءات) is the study of how best to collect, analyze, and draw conclusions from data.
Descriptive Statistics (إحصاء وصفي) summarizes qualities of a group (of people or things):

Using a Statistic / Parameter (إحصاءة) (mean, median, variance, range, ..etc.)
Numerical. It sums up variables across subjects to describe them in general.
Using a Graph / Plot (رسم بياني) (points, lines, colors, size, text, ..etc.)
Visual. They simplify the data by doing local statistics (e.g., bins of bar plot).

In both cases, we are interested in look at multipe statistics, and multiple graphs, before we move on to making conclusions.

title: Statistics
text:
Inferentail Statistics
Inferential Statistics (احصاء استدلالي) goes from description to drawing conclusions.

At its heart, statistics is the bridge between raw observation and useful information.

title: Statistics
text:
Lab

“involve me and I remember”

title: Statistics
text:
Counts
Statistics starts with Counts; the number of subjects with a certain characteristic.
Example is the value_counts(), summarizing the count of each value in the variable column:

penguins['island'].value_counts()

island
Biscoe       168
Dream        124
Torgersen     52
Name: count, dtype: int64


But we could also visualize it:

sns.displot(
    data=penguins, x='island', hue='island',
    shrink=0.8, discrete=True,
    height=3, aspect=1
)

title: Statistics
text:
Distributions
The Distribution of a variable is the frequencies of its values

For categorical variables (like island) they are just the proportions
For numerical variables, we need binning; i.e., grouping ranges of numerical values into buckets (we’ll see this when we plot it)

Let’s look at the NBA players dataset:

df = pd.read_csv('../../data/nba_heights.csv')
df.head()








last_name
first_name
h_meters
h_in

Let’s look at the value counts of height in meters h_meters (numerical):

counts = df['h_meters'].value_counts()
counts.nlargest(10)

h_meters
2.06    58
2.03    43
2.08    43
2.01    41
2.11    39
1.91    35
1.98    34
2.13    33
1.96    28
1.88    21
Name: count, dtype: int64


We describe a distribtuion by three properties:

Central Tendency
Dispersion
Skewness


1. Central Tendency

Mode: the most frequent value (\(\bar{x}\) or \(\mu\)).
Median: the middle value when data are ordered.
Mean (average): the center of “mass”.

The mean (\(\mu\)) is the sum of the values divided by their count:
\[
\mu = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i
\]

\(\mu\) denotes population mean
\(N\) is the count



2. Dispersion

Range: the difference between the maximum and minimum values.
Variance: the average of the squared differences from the mean (\(\sigma^2\)).
Standard Deviation: the square root of the variance (\(\sigma\)).

The variance (\(\sigma^2\)) is described mathematically as:
\[
\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \mu)^2
\]
In a physical sense, we can see:

the variance being the spread of the mass
the mean being the center of the mass




Physical Metaphor


In Pandas, we simply call the describe method on the column (series), to get all these statistics:

df['h_meters'].describe()

count    435.000000
mean       2.012483
std        0.090078
min        1.750000
25%        1.960000
50%        2.030000
75%        2.080000
max        2.290000
Name: h_meters, dtype: float64


Note the following:

The std is the standard deviation
The 50% percentile is the median statistic.
The range is shown by the max and min statistics.
The mode is more useful when we are looking at categorical attribute. In our case, the attribute is numerical.



Percentiles
The median is the 50th percentile (50%) which means that 50% of the data points are below this value.
The 25% and the 75% percentiles go by other names: - the 1st and 3rd quartiles - the Q1 and Q3 - the lower and upper quartiles


Visualizing the Distribution
We can visualize the count of their heights with a Histogram, which is one type of Distribution Plot; named as such because it shows where the values of the variable height lie on the x-axis, and how many of them are there (count).
Instead of showing all the values, we group them into Bins of uniform size, and represent how many values in the bin, vertically, by the height of bin itself.
This is the simplest and most useful type of statistical plot. In seaborn, it is displot (distribution plot), like so:

sns.displot(df, x='h_meters')








Sometimes we use the Kernel Density Estimation plot (KDE) instead to show the distrubtion:

sns.displot(df, x='h_meters', kind='kde', fill=True)

title: Introduction to Data Science
text:
Data Analysis

Data analysis is the process of inspecting, cleansing, transforming, and modeling data with the goal of discovering useful information, informing conclusions, and supporting decision-making.

Two approaches:

Confirmatory Data Analysis (CDA)
A top-down approach in which we start with a question and use data to answer it.
Example: Hypothesis: “people are addicted to smart phones”. Let’s see if the data supports this.
Lab 1: Blind Typing Experiment
Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)
A bottom-up approach in which we start with the data and try to find something interesting.
Example Data: “people who exercise at the gym”. Let’s find out what are their: ages, occupations, and common interests are. Lab 2: Exploring US Census 2010-2019 Data





Snow, John. 1855. On the Mode of Transmission of Cholera. Part 3, Table IX. Available at: http://www.ph.ucla.edu/epi/snow/snowbook3.html (accessed 26 June 2016); John Churchill. http://www.ph.ucla.edu/epi/snow/snowbook3.html.
